- “The Sovereign Skies: 5 Private Jets You Can Fly Yourself: Kind: captions Language: en Picture this. The runway shimmers under the morning sun. A soft hum rises from polished turbines. In the world of aviation, a quiet revolution has begun where billionaires, entrepreneurs, and visionaries no longer hire pilots. They become them. These are not the airliners of boardrooms and bodyguards. They are personal crowns crafted in metal, leather, and air. And today, we count down the five private jets that redefine sovereignty in the skies. Each designed for one purpose, to put total control into your hands. Let's begin where independence first meets luxury. Born in Brazil's innovation heartland, the Embraer Phenom 100 EV is where most pilots begin their ascent into the jet elite. Its silhouette sleek and confident. Its cabin lit by Embra's signature oval windows that flood the interior with daylight. At roughly $3 million, it's a ticket into a new realm of freedom. A compact powerhouse capable of cruising at over 400 mph, carrying seven passengers across 1,78 m. Just enough to skip from New York to Chicago without a single fuel stop. The cockpit is a technological cocoon powered by Garmin's G3000 avionics, touchscreens, smart automation, a co-pilot in code, and it sips fuel efficiently around 80 gall an hour, making it one of the most economical in its class. For those leaving piston aircraft behind, this jet feels like an initiation, a handshake between ambition and altitude. But for some, that isn't enough. They don't just want to fly. They want innovation written into their wings. Enter the Cirrus Vision Jet SF50 G2 Plus. The world's first true personal jet. It looks nothing like the rest. A single engine perched on its back, a Vtail cutting through clouds like a signature stroke. At 2 $75 million, it's the lightest, most accessible jet on this list. It flies 1/200 m at 300 mph, easily hopping between coasts or cities. But what sets it apart isn't speed, it's philosophy. This is the only jet in the world equipped with a whole aircraft parachute. Pull one handle and the entire jet floats safely to Earth. For new pilots, that changes everything. It replaces fear with confidence, turning every flight into freedom without compromise. The cockpit's perspective touch suite even senses when oxygen levels drop, triggering an automatic descent before you ever notice. It's thoughtful, human, beautifully cirrus. From Brazil to Minnesota, the dream of personal flight grows bolder. But to master the skies, you need more than accessibility. You need pedigree. And that's where the next jet steps in. A machine that has carried the world's elite for generations. Reborn for a single pilot's hand. The Cessna Citation M2 Gen 2. It's the symbol of aviation heritage. Twin engines, Jet Black Authority. Priced at nearly $6 million, the M2 Gen 2 is the bridge between personal flight and corporate command. It climbs to 41,000 ft above storms, above airliners at 460 mph. Inside, Garmin's triple screen flight deck gleams like a digital cathedral, reducing workload and amplifying safety. It's the jet that doesn't just fly, it endures decades of refinement. global service access and a lineage trusted by private pilots and Fortune 500 CEOs alike. And when you sit in its seat, you feel something subtle, a legacy in motion. But from here, the air thins and the stakes rise. Because the next aircraft on our list isn't built for comfort alone. It's built to land where no other jet dares, to turn dirt and gravel into runways, and isolation into luxury. The runway stretches out like brushed silver, but this time it isn't paved. Gravel, grass, even dirt. Most jets would tremble at the thought. But the Pilates PC24 doesn't just take off, it conquers. Built in the heart of Switzerland, where precision is a national art, the PC24 redefineses what's possible for a jet this size. Starting at just over $10 million, it's affectionately called the super versatile jet. And for good reason. It needs barely 2,900 ft of runway, half of what most jets require. That means private owners can land almost anywhere. Remote islands, mountain lodges, or estates hidden from the world's noise. Two Williams FJ44 engines roar behind it, pushing it to 440 mph with a range of nearly 2,000 mi. Inside, it's pure Pilates luxury. a flat floor cabin nearly 6 ft tall, handcrafted leather seating, and a silence that feels designed. It can carry up to 10 passengers or convert to a cargo layout for business operations. One day, it's a family retreat to the Alps. The next, a mobile boardroom over the Atlantic. This is not just aviation, it's freedom with a Swiss accent. But there's still one level above. A jet so precisely engineered that even gravity feels like an afterthought. A machine that fuses Japanese innovation with American craftsmanship. Born from the automotive dynasty of Honda. Enter the Honda Jet Elite 2. At first glance, it looks unlike any other jet. The engines mounted above the wings aren't a design quirk. They're a revelation. By removing the engines from the fuselage, Honda freed up cabin space, reduced drag, and quieted the interior until it feels almost sacred. It cruises at nearly 485 mph, reaching destinations over 1,500 m away. Yet, it remains intimate, perfect for a single pilot and six guests. Its takeoff distance just 3,900 ft. Climb rate 4,000 ft per minute. It slices through clouds like an arrow of light. Inside, the cabin feels more art gallery than aircraft. Every line is clean, every detail intentional. Soft LED lighting, whisper quiet engines, and a fully enclosed lavatory rare even among jets twice its size. The Garmin G3000 cockpit transforms the flight deck into a symphony of screens and synthetic vision. And beneath it all, GE Honda HF120 engines sip just 85 gall. Unheard of efficiency in this performance class. But numbers alone don't crown it king. What gives the Honda Jet its throne is its spirit. It's the embodiment of Honda's philosophy, the power of dreams. When founder Mitimasa Fujino first sketched its over-the-wing design on a napkin, many called it impossible. Today, it's one of the quietest, most efficient, most elegant private jets ever built. Imagine this moment. The engines hum, the runway glows beneath you, and the horizon bends to your will. When I was a child, I dreamed of being a policeman, a great policeman, a defender of everything. My childhood sidelined me. I was kicked out of my house by my mom. When I was 10 years old, I met some people on the street, they gave me their hand and that's how I started in drug trafficking. They sent me to a school, it's called a training school, where they send you to learn how to shoot, how to defend yourself, self-defense, how to measure your shot. It's like a cartel training school. From age 12 to 20 I was on patrol. After that, I was assigned as a Comanche. After Comanche, I was assigned to him, he took out a team carrying, caring for, and transporting people. Normally, when there was a boom in small planes here, it was mainly because they were used for advertising, something that doesn't happen anymore. It's over. It's very difficult to avoid detection if you stop because airplane engines generate a lot of heat . Then you stop and they see the heat. Look, this one stopped, it's going to continue in a bit. You take off again and they've already realized you're going in stages and it's the same thing. In other words, you're not leaving them. If they've already seen you and want to follow you, they'll follow you until you reach your destination. The only way is if you reach a mountainous area or a jungle where they can't catch you by land , that's why the planes are abandoned, and those on the ground colluding with them pick up the drugs, which is ultimately what matters to the cartel; the plane is no longer needed . Flying is something extraordinary. When I haven't flown in a long time and suddenly I get the chance to fly, I feel like I'm coming back to life. Here in Culiacán, the idea of being a pilot was very appealing because there were many small planes 10, 15 years ago. There are many pilots who are very skilled at business, at making money, and because they are so skilled, it lends itself to them doing business with the cartel and starting to work and earn their own money and grow, and you see them with money, you see them with nice houses, nice cars , but that is already, it's not going away, but you see it much less now. When I see students at the private pilot school, which is the one I attended, I point out to them that they are spending 13,000, 15,000, 16,000. That money they're spending is for a private pilot. To finish the lap. Moving drugs by small plane is indeed faster and less detectable than by land, because on land there are checkpoints, operations, and inspections; in other words, there is a greater probability that the merchandise will be lost by land than by air. There is no border, there is nothing to stop you, there are no customs, there are no customs, nobody checks you. And I had a knife with me and I said, "I'm gonna cut it." And he goes like, "No, no, don't cut my harness. Then I knew he was okay. And if I did, I was really hoping it was just for an engine out, you know, pilot who had to land in the middle of the field. - Brian shows up, and he took the vital signs. And that is what I was freaking out about, was his vital signs, 'cause his lips were blue, and he took his vital signs, and they were like 117 over 80. And I'm like, "Thank you, God." And his pulse was in the 90s. - And that's part of this sport is that there are injuries. There are incidents, they are rare. They are few and far between. It's not something that people should look at this sport and saying, "Wow, this is a very dangerous sport." 'Cause it's really not that dangerous. It's more pilot in control. And you take your eyes off the road while you're driving for just a few seconds, looking, texting, doing something you shouldn't be doing, and incidents happen, and accidents happen. And that's the same in the sport. And we're very lucky that that it was just, as I understand it, a broken femur, broken tib-fib fracture, maybe something in the hip on one side, and he'll be fine. He'll probably be up and flying again in a few months. - Yeah, it's just very, very sad. It doesn't really change anything for the trip as we need to keep going. We've got an email from Joo saying, "Just keep going. Don't worry about me. I'll be fine. And he wants everybody to have a good time." But at the end, we missed a partner, and a great friend, and we wish him good recovery, and as fast as possible. But we have to keep going. There's all that group here put together. And we're all here for the same goal, reaching the Gulf of Mexico. Right now we're just at the beginning of the trip, a lot more things could happen. And we just learned from what happened that don't fly in the wrong direction, and don't fly too close to the ground. Just be careful, very, very important. We just want arrive there safe. - [Narrator] The day's events come as a shocking reminder that nothing is certain, but change. In the aftermath, the pilots come together to socialize and connect. Many of them are still strangers. However, the tragic crash has bonded the group and forced each pilot to lean on the others. They'll need a collective focus to surmount the true dangers and harsh consequences along this adventure. - Playing petanque. - The perfect day tomorrow is everybody wakes up with a smile. I really love to see a little bit of wind help us out. So we don't have to run as far. Tape measure to measure the distance between the winner and the loser. And everybody kinda get up in the air and just see us look like a flock of birds up there. And it'd be nice and smooth, so I can take it all in, take some pictures of my friends who are up there in the air. It was hard. Dad got put into a home here in the past week. So that was kinda difficult. Kinda felt like I've failed a little bit, 'cause I was a caretaker, but everybody's reassured me that he needed to go into a facility, so... - [Narrator] As the next day begins, there is a stark contrast to the high energy of the day before. All is quiet for the morning's launch. And though on the outside, things seem to have settled, the terrain is always changing. Because of this constant terrestrial evolution, there is an active need for us to change with it. The Rio Grande is a priceless resource, the lifeblood to all those who live along its 1800-mile path. To better understand the role this river has played in this region, we speak with restoration and ecology expert, Heather Dutton. - By 1890, the waters of the Rio Grande were over-appropriated. Meaning we had more people here wanting to use water than there was water available. The over-appropriated nature of this river impacts everything we do. It impacts the ability for agriculture to be viable. We are experiencing impacts from climate change, dust on snow, large-scale tree die-offs, and potential impacts to watershed health. Those issues aren't unique to us. They are being experienced in watersheds all over Colorado, and all over the West. What I do think is unique and what's very energizing to me is that the challenges of the river bring us together, because we recognize that we have to work together. There's a finite amount of water, and there's a lot of different people that rely on this river and that want to make a living, either from diverting water, and taking it out into the closed basin and growing potatoes with it, or being able to see wildlife or go fishing. And so that's why it's so important for us to come together and think about the ways that we're managing the water, and how we can really stretch every last drop, so that we can have the greatest benefit for the people and the animals and the ecosystems that rely on the river. - [Narrator] The waters of the earth move in a system. Each body of water combines with others to create a larger whole. Every moves together with one ultimate goal, unity. - We are we're at about 9,000 feet, which means we're 200 feet off the deck. - And just (whooshing). (engine huming) - The flight was beautiful. It was great. It was nice and smooth. Mainly my first time flying over the clouds, or in the clouds. - [Stuart] In the clouds- - That line along the cliff. - When we had that one pass, I went to 1500, and then a few times I dropped down to like maybe 700 or something like that. Its flow has created a truly amazing landscape in an otherwise dry and barren desert. Today, it's little more than a shadow of its former self. Irrigation and other human uses have reduced its flow until it no longer floods in a natural cycle. - Climate change, it seems like we're getting less snow in the winter. We get enough water right now to make a crop, but it does go dry every year. Actually it makes it hard 'cause a lot of our water comes from the Rio Grande, but it does get up north. It does get diverted into canals, and we slowly get it down here. - [Narrator] In recent decades, agriculture, construction of dams, growth of cities and industry along the Rio Grande have diverted huge amounts of water. - Runoff from the mountains up north. You know, they store water in the reservoirs up there. And if we do get a snow pack, and if they don't get much of a snow pack well we're kinda stuck with what we have this year, not much water, you know, and then we run, the farmers run short. It makes it a little difficult. - [Narrator] Without dramatic changes, it seems unlikely that the Rio Grande will ever fully regain its former strength or impact on an ecosystem it created. At a refueling stop south of Hatch, New Mexico, once again, we find Stuart having problems with his engine. - Well, my kill switch wire coming off of my solenoid, broke in flight. So when I came down, I couldn't kill the motor. I'm trying to see if I can repair it before everybody takes off again, and I miss out on the next flight. So on every one of my refuels, I have flooded the engine, and not been able to take off. So I was gonna try to correct that on this one, but because of the kill switch would not work, I had to flood the engine in order to kill it. So I could be derailed here again. But all I can do is try and see what happens. - [Narrator] It can be challenging to manage your emotions at times in a sport of such high intensity. The stress can become magnified when traveling cross country in a group, as mechanical problems can cause a pilot to miss the next launch, as we have already seen for him. Aware that keeping up with the other pilots would be hard, it has been Stuart's lack of mechanical skills that have presented the biggest challenge for him. He has struggled to keep his motor tuned properly at altitude. He fouled the engine in Espanola, replaced the starter in Albuquerque, and now is having flooding problems after repairing the damaged kill switch wire. It can be difficult to keep a cool head in these moments, and remember to center yourself as a pilot. Fortunately, Stuart has had many years of high-intensity sport outside of paramotoring to fall back on, and is able to collect himself, address the problem by changing a spark plug and get airborne just in time to stay with the team. He's still keeping up for now. As the late morning thermal start to build, and the desert air becomes unstable, the pilots arrive at a truck stop north of El Paso, near the river. [Stuart] To be above those clouds with all the other pilots and the sun coming up over the horizon, it took my breath away. But as soon as you get above the clouds, it's all sunshine. It was a deep moment. It's definitely deep, you know. Looking around, but we are such a small thing in a big universe. Seeing the sunrise come up through these clouds, it was just like, "Oh." - I loved when there was a space open there for that. And you could see the ground kinda just going down through that space, oh my God, that was so bitchin. I'll never forget that. I keep thinking about that. That was great. - [Narrator] Having descended from the heavens, the pilot's refuel in a grocery store parking lot, and take off to complete the second morning leg of the day. As they fly along the border entry points, they're close enough to smell the salted ocean air. Excitement is brewing, but then, the rains of misfortune fall upon them once again. Although he's a seasoned pilot, for Jean Francois, his latest landing is not a good one. My foot was dangling, because I was out of my harness, was behind me more than normal, but it didn't go well, because just at the time I was going to land, I guess I got a little gust of wind. So everybody landed just fine. I was the only one that had some issues. Wind shifting, fully-loaded, landing with speed, and whatever happened happened, you just finish on the knees, that's all it is. Knee pads are the best. (laughing) And apparently, I broke a bone. That's what it seems like, like sprang ankle, and broken bone, just for a little impact, and they were all worried about me thinking, "What happened?" It looked fine, but just a little impact was just too much. Mistake, I don't know what happened. Yeah. I'm kind of bummed. I would like to fly with you guys tomorrow. - [Narrator] As the journey has promised to lead them, it is here, in the Gulf of Mexico, near South Padre Island, Texas, where the great river known as the Rio Grande finally meets it's end. This is where the water has flowed for 1800 miles to unite with the ocean in an inescapable harmony of life. This is also where our team's journey together concludes. - Coming in through that last bit of the river as it met the ocean, seeing the Rio Grande merged together with the ocean was just... I was like, "Yes, we made it." But at the same time, I was like sad that the trip is over. That means we're at the end. And I kinda just kept going out into the ocean. - Flying with 15 people, crew and pilots, going through all this and the adventures and the accidents and the crashes and the broken ankles and everything in the comradery with everybody to actually reach the end and with the group of people and be able to see the ocean and fly there and say, "I made it." I mean, that was incredible. That was just a bucket list item that I didn't even know was on my bucket list. - I just can't believe it's over. This has been an epic journey. This has been an amazing adventure. And it's been a great team of pilots to do it with. I wouldn't trade it for the world. This is where it all ends. But yeah, it's beautiful. Everything is... Yeah, it was awesome. It really was. - I'm wore out. (laughing) It was tough. It was really, really challenging, and... But I'm so jazzed, I got it. You guys did it. I didn't think I would make it to be honest with you. (laughing) So this one's for you, Dad. - [Narrator] So what can we determine from following water? These pilots have learned much in this adventure about themselves and the river. Perhaps it is that the real Grande is much more than snowmelt running from the rocky mountains to the Atlantic Ocean. Perhaps it's that this river is a natural artery, a central source of life to all who share its banks, and a reflection of the people who tend it's delicate balance along the way. But perhaps, like the water's natural connection to all life, the most important lesson is how much we all need each other. This simple lesson our world seems to need now more than ever. - The Economics of Flying Private: How NetJets Became the Uber of Private Jets: Kind: captions Language: en What does it actually cost to fly private? And why are more people doing it than ever before? Private aviation is a multibillion dollar business. And companies like NetJets are changing how the ultra wealthy move. Even in the smallest aircraft type in our program, you're talking today about an initial capital investment of more than $800,000 to buy a piece of an airplane. We have something like 40% of the Fortune 500 that are are NetJets customers. And as major commercial airlines are trying to win back premium customers, can commercial aviation companies successfully keep them? [Music] NetJets doesn't sell private jets. It sells fractions of jets. Customers buy a share as little as 116th of a specific aircraft model. That idea splitting jet ownership like a time share unlocked a new market. Today we operate nearly,00 aircraft which if you were comparing us to a commercial airline would make us the second or third largest on the planet. Unlike charter services, it owns and controls its fleet allowing tighter control over quality scheduling and safety. The average NetJets customer flies with us for about 75 to 100 hours per year. Our typical customer will be somebody who is a principal of a privately held company. We have something like 40% of the Fortune 500 that are our NetJets customers. Flying with NetJets doesn't come cheap. A fractional ownership share on a light jet like the Phenom 300 starts at around $850,000 upfront, plus about $325,000 per year in operating costs for 50 flight hours. For those who want a more affordable and flexible option, the NetJets card starts at $215,000 for 25 hours annually. That $215,000 jet card, that's the cost of four down payments on the average American home. Within the shared ownership model, we really have three cost components for the customer. You have the initial capital investment, which is a onetime investment where somebody's buying a piece of the airplane and then they pay a monthly management fee. If you owned your own airplane, you'd have pilot salaries, you need to have mechanic salaries, and you'd have hanger rent. So in our model, you're sharing that across the other members of the program. And then every time you fly, we bill you the variable hourly costs associated with flying that trip from A to B, which covers things like the fuel expense of the aircraft, the ground charges and the handling fees and the things that that would go into operating an airplane from A to B. That business model caught the attention of Warren Buffett. And in 1998, Berkshire Hathaway bought NetJets and has held on to it since. You see in the luxury travel segment overall, people are making more of an investment in experiences than in things. That certainly has been a tailwind for us. You know, if I'm being honest, the commercial experience has not gotten any better over the last several years, and that has driven more people to want to fly netjets. During the pandemic, demand for private air travel soared as people feared crowded airports and packed planes. In the US, business aviation traffic in 2021 hit record highs, surpassing the 2019 levels, and in 2021 saw over 3.3 million private jets worldwide, a 7% increase over preandemic levels. Only about 10% of the people who met the financial, you know, norms or averages of private aviation were actually flying privately. and CO certainly unlocked some portion of that addressable market. And then the big question was would they go back to the airlines when the pandemic subsided and you know masks came off and vaccines went in arms. After the initial co boom many private airline companies saw slumps in profits but in Q1 of 2025 Berkshire Hathaway reported a profit bump in its service division which it contributed to its aviation services. For NetJets, we're operating at 40 45% larger than we were in 2019. So, we've maintained record retention rates. Major commercial airlines are trying to claw back premium travelers by rolling out redesigned business class cabins, faster boarding, and high-end lounges. But for some flyers, the best perks aren't enough. Anonymity is key to our business. One of the appeals of flying netjets versus owning your own airplane is that you're virtually untraceable. Whether you have concerns about appearances or whether you have concerns about safety, netjets can provide that in a way that that you can't get owning your own airplane. As the demand for private flights increases, so does the visibility of their impact. Each trip emits many times more carbon emissions per passenger than a commercial trip, and that's drawn growing scrutiny, especially as more celebrities and CEOs are called out for frequent short hall trips. When Taylor Swift's private jet usage was tracked online in 2023, it sparked a wave of backlash and highlighted the growing divide between convenience and climate responsibility. Now with the business booming, even the industry's biggest players are under pressure to address the carbon footprint. Sustainability is at the forefront for our industry right now. And the way that we're addressing it at NetIts is we are the largest buyer of sustainable aviation fuel in the industry. We offer carbon offset programs for our customers. We offset all of our own internal company flying. So anything that we do for maintenance flights or training flights or anything like that, we we buy the carbon credits to offset. As commercial travel faces continued pressure from rising costs to growing delays, demand for more efficient discrete options isn't likely to vanish. Private aviation remains a niche but profitable business fueled by clients who value control more than cost.

- The Economics of Private Jets: Kind: captions Language: en This video was made possible by Curiosity Stream. Watch over 2,400 documentaries for free for a month at CuriosityStream.com/Wendover. Private jets represent an inconceivable level of opulence. If the average American were to spend their entire year’s salary to charter a Gulfstream G550 from New York, they would just barely make it to Utah, and yet, there’s a class of people who use these planes to fly not just from New York to Utah, but rather routes like New York to Beijing. There’s a class of people who will spend tens or hundreds of thousands of dollars on just one single flight. Now, there are a few reasons this is strange beyond just the sheer price of things. Chartering that Gulfstream from London to Dubai, for example, you’d end up paying about $55,000 at rack rate. Meanwhile, if you were to fly Emirates First Class, which is just, if not more opulent, you could fly between the cities twenty times for the same price. What’s even stranger about this kind of expense is that businesses, which are intended to maximize profits for their shareholders, are able to justify this enormous expense as worthwhile. So, when is it that paying $8,000 or more an hour to fly makes money? There’s a pretty simple way of figuring this out. Out of the 8,760 hours in a year, the average CEO works 2,716 of them. For a CEO that’s paid $1 million a year, that makes an hour of their time worth $368. Among America’s largest companies, though, the average CEO earns $15.6 million. That makes their hour worth $5,750. For the most part, though, private jets fly about the same speed as commercial planes so when flying a route like London to Dubai, the time savings come at the airports on each end. It comes from being able to arrive, get on a plane, and fly—rather than having to navigate one’s way through a busy terminal for a fixed flight time. But still, flying private versus commercial from London to Dubai would save, at most, about three hours in airport time. With the cost of $55,000 for the flight, that would mean the CEO’s time would have to be worth $18,300 per hour. That wouldn’t be true until they earned $50 million a year—a salary earned only by the upper echelon of CEO’s. But the truth is that, for the most part, private jets do not make economic sense when flying routes with plenty of commercial service like London to Dubai. One of the larger corporate jet fleets out there belongs to Walmart. Now, this might come as a surprise considering that this is a company so focused on keeping costs low. You see, Walmart is headquartered in Bentonville, AR—a relatively small city of 50,000. Their airport does have a surprising amount of service for such a small city with flights all the way to Los Angeles and New York, largely propped up the company’s traffic, but for the higher ups, commercial doesn’t cut it. That’s why the company has a fleet of 20 corporate jets—the largest of any American company. These are most frequently flown by the company’s Regional Vice Presidents who are in charge of a specific area of the country and will have to make frequent store visits within this region. The company apparently has a goal that nobody spends a night away from Bentonville—they want as many trips as possible to be day trips. Now, let’s say that one of these executive vice presidents needs to take a trip to three stores—one in Rock Springs, Wyoming; one in Spokane, Washington; and the last in Great Falls, Montana. Getting to Rock Springs requires a seven hour itinerary through Denver that would get this executive in at 9:30 pm therefore already requiring an overnight stop. Then, the next day, they would do their store visit in the morning and, as there are only two flights a day from Rock Springs, they would have to wait until 4:50 pm to catch a flight back to Denver then another one to Spokane, getting in at 8:30 pm local time, therefore requiring another overnight stop. The next morning they would do their site visit, but once again, flight schedules dictate that the first itinerary to Great Falls would leave at 5:05 pm through Salt Lake City, getting in at 10:04 pm local time, thereby requiring another overnight stop. Following the next morning’s store visit, this executive would catch a noon flight to Denver and, after a three hour layover, another to finally get into Bentonville at 8pm. These three store visits would therefore take up four whole working days, but what if this executive flew private? Leaving at 9am, the first flight direct to Rock Springs would take an hour and 45 minutes getting in, with the hour’s time change, at 9:45 am local time. After a two hour store visit, the plane would take off again at 11:45 am, flying an hour and 15 minutes to Spokane, getting in at noon local time. After another two hour store visit, the plane would take off at 2 pm for a quick 45 minute flight to Great Falls, getting in at 3:45 pm local time. After a final two hours at this store, the plane would take off its final time at 5:45 pm bound for Bentonville. 2 hours and 15 minutes of flight time later, it would land at 9 pm local time, exactly 12 hours after leaving. What was a four day trip on commercial flights becomes a day trip on private, and that’s why Walmart decided private jets are worth it for them. It’s all about valuing the time of their employees and they’ve determined that, even for the relatively low level vice presidents, their time is valuable enough that it’s worth flying them private. For example, one of the aircraft Walmart owns and operates is the Learjet 45. It costs the company about $4 per mile to operate this aircraft including crew, fuel, insurance, maintenance, and all other variable costs. Therefore, the 2,900 miles flown on that day-trip to the north-east would cost them about $11,600. Saving three days, that places a value of $3,900 per day which means that, assuming the executive onboard works every single one of the 260 workdays per year, they would have to make almost exactly $1 million per year for this private jet ride to be worth it to Walmart—an amount within the realm of possibility for upper management at such a large company. Of course, that’s not factoring in the alternative option’s hotel, food, and airline transport costs which would likely sum in the thousands and it’s also assuming there’s just one passenger. If the plane were to be filled to its maximum capacity of nine, each passenger would only need to be paid $111,000 per year for the expense to be worth it to the company which is less than an average Walmart store manager makes. Now, there’s one other case where private jets can make economic sense over flying commercial. Let’s say Walmart was looking to expand into the Philippines. Flying business class, it would cost a minimum of $5,000 roundtrip per person, require three stops, and take over 26 hours to get from Bentonville to Manila. Flying private, though, a long range jet like the Bombardier Global 7500 could make it there non-stop, in just 15 hours, carrying 19 of the company’s top executives. Since the company does not own this type of jet, it would likely charter one at a cost of about $10,000 an hour, or $150,000 for the trip. While the cost of commercial airfare is less than this, assuming the CEO, who makes $24 million a year, is onboard, the value of the eleven hours of his time saved is worth $97,000—clearly tilting the math in favor of the private jet. The general phenomenon of globalization has been great for the private jet market as businesses need to travel to far off places like this. Especially as companies outsource manufacturing and other operations into developing countries, which don't necessary have much air service, many companies have determined that private jets are the best way to get where they need to go. But despite this, the private aviation industry was hit hard but the global financial crisis and still has not fully recovered. While part of it was genuine cost cutting, businesses also wanted to show that they were doubling down on luxuries by getting rid of their jets, even if they could make economic sense in some cases. It was all about optics and nowadays, these jets are coming with poorer and poorer optics, for good reason. Private jets are truly horrendous for the environment. If one were to fly that Bombardier Global 7500, the one that could make it from Bentonville to Manila, with just one passenger onboard, the jet would make it only to South Dakota before that passenger’s carbon footprint exceeded that of the average person in one year. Increasingly, these jets are even being used for purposes that cannot be justified economically. Since 2013, there has been about a 10% increase among Fortune 100 executives of using their company’s corporate jets for personal, leisure purposes. They apparently justify this by saying that, in case of a work emergency, they might need to get back to the office quickly and commercial air travel could hinder that. Firms that include this as a perk for their executives, according to one set of research, under-perform against the market average in terms of shareholder returns by about 4% each Of course, the real reason some companies might have private jets is not because it makes economic sense, because it quite often doesn’t. It’s because the people who decide whether the firm will use these are the very people that will use them. In many instances, the explanation is not economic, it’s societal. Now, many Wendover Productions viewers are also probably the type of person who watches a lot of documentaries. If that’s you or if you’d like that to be you, you should absolutely be subscribed to CuriosityStream. That’s because they have a library of over 2,400 documentaries and non-fiction titles that you can watch whenever, wherever.   8. @theophrastus3.056: A good analysis for the most part. However the initial calculations (later corrected) are for a single CEO making solo trips. In reality, he’s usually accompanied by several other execs, also making top dollar. 
       The value of a corporate jet doing in one day what would take several days on the airlines is spot on. However, the flight time on the private aircraft is also work time. There’s no privacy or a cabin setup that makes this possible on the airlines. 
      Also, there’s security: Imagine an airline hijacker getting ahold of a Walton, Musk or Gates as a hostage!
     Finally, there’s fractional ownership, as with Warren Buffet’s NetJets and their nearly 1000-plane fleet. The fixed cost are divided between many owners. And trips can be done on a jet appropriate to the mission: light, mid-size or intercontinental range. And with even greater anonymity.

  10. @disgraced-empire: This video misses a key reason for flying private, it’s not just about savings the airport hours. It’s about having the flight be at whatever time you want, therefore allowing the executives to have any amount of meetings or sales calls that they want without conforming to a commercial air schedule. That is by far the biggest reason corporations choose private flights
  11. @jeremyleonbarlow: The economics are complex. I mean if you are living in Los Angeles and you have 6 or 7 buddies and you all want to fly to Vegas for the weekend, you can rent a light jet to fly you all collectively to Vegas and back for about the same cost as a commercial round trip ticket, give or take a couple of hundred dollars each per ticket. I mean the complexity of commercial ticket pricing gives a lot of wiggle room, but a realistic rental price for 8 friends flying LA to Vegas round trip on a light jet is between $500 and $600 each.

Renting a long range heavy jet or super mid-size private jet for a lengthy trip between cities for a business isn't just about the time savings for a single person in management typically. I mean a 15 or 20 seat private business jet probably has at a minimum ten people flying on it, so you need to factor in the price differential between X number of tickets and the costs of chartering or using a company owned jet, and what is more given fixed costs of an aircraft, even with high up front costs, owning a jet might be more economical still. This is because there are ways to improve the value of an owned aircraft which allows you to factor more than just the value of those folks' time savings at aurports. You must add to that calculus the fact that many businesses and individuals also subsidize company or personal travel on a private jet by triple net leasing their owned jet to a charter service with a deal that allows them 10 or more hours per month of usage at cost. This type of leasing arrangement is often through a service that allows them to use a network of jets in the charter service's fleet. This helps obscure travel by the company, protecting proprietary information in many instances better than traditional private ownership. I mean Taylor Swift and Elon Musk's tail numbers were being tracked as I recall and Sergei Brin and Larry Page decided chartering was more economical for this exact reason. Competitors knowing your next move by tracking where you flew is not a good thing, but owning and using a networked leaseback arrangement can be more economical in addition to obscuring your travel.

These leasing arrangements maximize cash flow on the jet as an asset to both the charter operator and the lessor by allowing the jets to keep moving and avoid a fixed base of operation. This maximizes charter revenue. This reduces the costs further for the owner of the jet by spreading fixed costs over more hours of usage and offloading those costs onto chartered guests. I mean with amortization and depreciation, a company which might have spent $7.5 million flying private over the course of five years, after the savings associated with the right charter leaseback arrangement, could have in fact spent just $360k for the same 600 hours of flight time. At what becomes $600 per hour for the flight time in such an arrangement, owning a private company jet makes a whole lot more sense.

Now it isn't always going to allow the company or individual to reduce costs that much, as some unexpected maintenance items might raise costs. I mean one cracked windshield could costs tens of thousands of dollars to replace, not to mention lost revenue from the loss of flight time while waiting for the repair, but it limits downside of ownership, and can still make flying private far more economical than traditional chartering. Of course chartering still might be cost effective for businesses or individuals who fly less frequently because of the value of time savings alone. This is why high usage owners can subsidize their travel costs.

- The reality of owning your own airplane: Kind: captions Language: en after five years of owning a cessna 182 here's my honest take on the really fun things and also the hard things about it the first thing i noticed and it's remained consistent throughout the last five years is the sense of adventure you have when you own an airplane and so this might sound obvious but but for a lot of people that don't yet fly they can probably think hey is that overrated is it an exaggeration that you can just kind of go wherever whenever and i can answer that for you it's not an exaggeration with very few restrictions you can go just about wherever you want and experience some really cool things so i've been able to experience the backcountry mountains of idaho which are just captivating i've seen really cool views of state and national parks here in texas out in west texas be able to explore the underrated parts of northwestern arkansas just the beauty that that part of the country holds and so many other things because of having an airplane so it's really not an exaggeration of what you're able to do even in a small airplane and so even though it doesn't go that fast it's a heck of a lot faster than driving and i like to say that hey my 182 will take you anywhere you want to go in the country pretty comfortably if you can just sit there long enough but i also quickly realized that along with all the adventure and newfound freedom that you get owning an airplane also comes all the unexpected scenarios the unexpected maintenance and and to a large degree i already expected these i mean um that stuff just happens but so it's one thing to expect it and it's another thing to actually experience it and think oh man i'm i'm nowhere near my hometown and this went wrong so i'm kind of stuck here until i can get this fixed those situations have happened i've never gotten real out of position but it's kind of just something you're always always dealing with and and stuff will come up so not not a deal breaker by any means but it's a very real thing that happens so if you're going to buy an airplane you have to just kind of expect to the unexpected which i don't really love that saying but but it's turned out to be very very true after flying it for about 50 to uh really even like 100 hours i was really surprised by how much my skills had grown in my competence in that particular airplane more so than i expected it's one thing to have a hundred hours in a certain type of airplane it's another thing to have a hundred hours in the exact same airplane because all the avionics are the same you get to go through different procedures and different types of flying but it's all in the same airplane all the switches are in the same place all the you know landing kind of feels the same and everything so i was able to progress a lot more in 100 hours of flying the same airplane than 100 hours of flying a bunch of other types of airplanes and things so that's one really really rewarding and fun part about owning your own airplane is that you can become really really good at it in a way that it's just harder to do when you're kind of renting different types of airplanes so once i had a few years under my belt i could look back and start to think about expenses because i didn't want to overreact in year one if expenses were higher on maintenance or you know whatever and so once i had kind of a few years worth of data to work with i can confirm that yes flying is expensive you're going to have you're going to more expensive years you're going to have less expensive years but it's all expensive i purposely haven't calculated the hourly cost but i uh i figure it's probably 150 to 200 or so for the 182 depending on how much i fly um but my outlook on expenses really comes down to three things one is that i'm never gonna skimp on maintenance of the ways to get hurt in aviation this is so so avoidable and so if i can't afford the maintenance on the airplane i'm not going to fly i'm not going to own the airplane that's a deal breaker for me the second thing is that i want to live within my means i want to spend less than i make and then the third kind of principle to live by financially i believe is to take the remaining the residual um time energy effort resources that you have and then and then allocate it in places uh that are that are important to you and so if flying can fit into all of those three things and remain consistent then i think for me flying has been a green light so yes some years are more expensive than others but as long as those principles can hold true for me then i try not to sweat it too much one of the most fun parts of flying for me might actually surprise you because as awesome as exploring the mountains of idaho or the different parts of the country can be really one of the most rewarding moments has been the unspectacular ones when you're flying across central texas which isn't much to look at but it allows you the chance to think and think deeply about how grateful i am to live in a time and a place of human history where this is even possible i try never to get bored in the cockpit because i'm so grateful to have that opportunity and to enjoy you know looking at the world thousands of feet above it going 150 miles an hour and it's just it's just awesome so oddly enough that's been one of the most fun parts of flying for me has been just a deep sense of gratitude that i've been able to develop in the unspectacular moments owning an airplane has also been a really practical tool for me my family lives hundreds of miles away so anytime i go visit them having the airplane is a really really useful part of that process but along with that one of the downsides comes stress so anytime you're counting on the airplane or counting on the weather to cooperate it can add stress anytime one of those things isn't quite cooperating and there's been times where i i fly down there the weather is great and it's time to come home the weather sucks and so you might have to get out of position because you might borrow a car or something and drive all the way home and then you got to reverse the process to get back into position first world problems i know but uh it's a useful tool until it isn't and then there's some stress involved with that but that's been more than worth it for me i think it's kind of part of the fun and one thing that is both fun and challenging has been the timing of expenses one huge shift that i've noticed from renting an airplane moving over to owning an airplane has been when you actually pay for flying when you rent you know you only pay when you're using it which has an upside because you can kind of control the expenses up and down but it has a downside because anytime you want to just go fly and watch the sunset you get a bill for how much that costs you just go watch that sunset and it's like oh that's kind of a harsh way to think about it but in owning the opposite is true because the the best days are the days where you already have a full tank gas you go fly for a few hours and you put the airplane up and you didn't spend a penny at least not not the way that it felt that day but then the opposite is true when the airplane might be at in in the shop for a while or weather sucks for a while but you're still paying for the hanger and you're still paying for all the other stuff and you're like well i spent a lot of money on that and didn't really get to use it as much so just the timing of expenses is a different dynamic it's got pros and cons sometimes you love it sometimes uh you kind of miss renting in that regard but that that's been something that's just taken some getting used to so while i've learned these things over five years of owning an airplane i have put together my thoughts of things that i've learned over 16 years of flying in general and you can get those over at airplaneacademy.com join i put together a five day free email series where i put together my five most impactful lessons that i've learned as an aviator over the last 16 years and i want to share those with you i hope that it'll serve you in your aviation journey so airplaneacademy.com join we'll get you signed up for free and i'd love to get to know you a little bit better through email and i look forward to seeing you there

jets long considered the ultimate symbol of luxury and exclusivity but the truth is they're not as straightforward to acquire as you might think in fact it's a process fraught with challenges and complexities especially in today's world with growing environmental concerns the Journey Begins With identifying your unique aircraft requirements after all one size does not fit all in the world of private Aviation are you a frequent traveler or do you need a jet for occasional trips knowing your needs is crucial to avoid overbuying or under utilizing your jet next up setting a budget private jets come in various price ranges from used to brand new it's essential to decide how much you're willing to spend before proceeding remember used aircraft can be significantly more budget-friendly making them a practical choice for many buyers once you've determined your budget it's time to consider how you'll Finance your jet whether it's through cash loans or lines of credit you'll need a solid financial plan in place paying attention to interest rates and the fine print is critical in today's high rate environment making an offer is where the excitement builds negotiating a price can be a strategic game especially when buying used aircraft it's important to know your bargaining power and be prepared for counter offers but remember if someone offers more you might lose the aircraft you desire and finally the moment you've been waiting for taking delivery of your private jet if you buy directly from a manufacturer the process is fairly straightforward however on the second hand Market delivery Logistics can be more complex you might need to coordinate Pilots maintenance and more and so there you have it the step-by-step guide to buying a private jet it's a journey that involves careful planning budgeting negotiation and Logistics but for those who dream of soaring the skies in their own aircraft it's a journey worth taking if you found this video helpful or have हाँ, एक बार। तो तुम्हें यकीन है कि ये प्लेन हमारे लिए है? हाँ, सर। ऐसा लग रहा है जैसे मैं पूरा एक मंज़िल ऊपर जा रहा हूँ। ये मुझे ज़मीन पर रखने का अच्छा तरीका है। लेकिन डेनवर मुझे देखकर बहुत खुश होगा। असल में, मैं तुम्हें डेनवर के बारे में कुछ बताता हूँ। डेनवर में धरती को चपटा मानने वाले बहुत लोग हैं, भाई। वहीं पर फ्लैट अर्थ कन्वेंशन हुआ था जिसमें हम घुस गए थे। और हमें पता चला कि लगभग 60% डेनवर-वासी सोचते हैं कि धरती चपटी थी। डेनवर-वासी? डेनवेराइट्स नहीं? मुझे लगता है कि शायद डेनवेराइट्स है। जेक की प्रेस कॉन्फ्रेंस अभी एंथनी जोशुआ के साथ चल रही है। मैं देख रहा हूँ और मैं बस इलॉन मस्क को शाउट आउट देना चाहता हूँ, यार इलॉन मस्क मुझे पता है तुम मेरे व्लॉग्स देखते हो दोस्त। शायद तुम्हें पता न हो पर हाँ, वो बहुत बड़ा फैन है। उसने मेरे व्लॉग्स देखे हैं, वो सब्सक्राइब करने को कहता है, लाइक और सब्सक्राइब। हाँ, पर ये बात कि मैं इस लाइव स्ट्रीम का मज़ा 43,000 फीट की ऊँचाई पर ले पा रहा हूँ। स्टारलिंक का शुक्रिया। ये सच में कमाल का लगता है। मैं उसका चेहरा तोड़ दूँगा, मैं उसका शरीर तोड़ दूँगा। मैं उसे पूरी तरह कुचल दूँगा। मुझे उस पर यकीन नहीं है। वैसे, वो कौन सा विरोधी है जिससे तुम्हें इस हफ्ते लड़ना था? वो जेक पॉल के कैंप में है। कौन है वो? कैसियस। ठीक है। उसकी तो पहले ही धुलाई कर चुका हूँ। माफ़ करना, कैसियस। रुको, क्या ये वही बंदा है जिससे वो स्पारिंग कर रहा था? अभी उसका घुटना टूटा हुआ है। हे भगवान, क्या तुमने वो सुना? रुको, उसे उस बंदे से लड़ना था जिसे हमने जेक के साथ स्पारिंग करते देखा था। ये तो बहुत ही गज़ब है। ओ, यार। असल में, बाजू लगभग एक ही साइज़ के हैं। जोशुआ थोड़ा डरा हुआ लग रहा है। हाँ, वो फोकस्ड नहीं लग रहा है। बिल्कुल। मेगाफाइट, जेक पॉल, आई लव यू। आई लव यू और तुम मुझे प्रेरणा देते हो, यार। वो मुझे रोमन रेंस, कोडी रोड्स, उसोज़ और सीएम पंक की धुलाई करने के लिए प्रेरित करता है। 29 नवंबर, सैन डिएगो में मिलते हैं। वॉर गेम्स! ये! तीन, चार, वाह। उन सीढ़ियों से नीचे उतरने में लगभग एक वाइन जितना समय लगा। कमाल है। छे सेकंड। वैसे, वाइन वापस आ रहा है। उसका नाम डिवाइन है। मैं विज़न को सपोर्ट करने डेनवर आया हूँ। अपनी गैंग को सपोर्ट तो करना ही पड़ेगा। ओह, गैंग, हम वापस आ गए। ये बंदा हमारे साथ है। यस, सर। मुझे बहुत भूख लगी है। ओह, वो देखो। ये एकदम सही है, मुझे उन लोगों को शाउट आउट देना होगा जिन्होंने ये ब्रांड बनाया, जैसे मुझे भूख लगी है, आसान छोटा झटपट खाना। तुम लोग वाकई बहुत सोचते हो। ओह, रुको, ये मैं हूँ। ब्रूस प्रिचर्ड, बस एक आदमी... जिसे मैं देखना चाहता था। तुम मुझे देख सकते हो? अब तुम मुझे नहीं देख सकते। हे, बढ़िया था ब्रूस, क्या तुम यहाँ WWE जेट, लाल वाले जेट से आए हो? हाँ। अच्छा लगता होगा। हाँ, अच्छा लगता है। मुझे पता होगा। बिल्कुल वैसे ही वाले से आया हूँ, वही साइज़, सब कुछ। लेकिन तुम्हारी कुर्सी मेरी जितनी आरामदायक नहीं होगी। तुम्हारे पास कश्मीरी है? क्या तुम्हारे, क्या तुम, उह,भी कश्मीरी की लाइन में हैं? तो ये वाला, ये वाला नहीं था। नहीं, इसमें कोई कश्मीरी नहीं है। ये किराए का है। तकनीकी रूप से ये किराए का है। उबर जैसा है। क्या तुम मेरे वीडियो ब्लॉग के लिए कोई थीम सॉन्ग या कुछ चाहते हो? क्या मुझे थीम सॉन्ग चाहिए? ये है ब्रूस प्रिचर्ड, या! इसके पास कश्मीरी सीट हैं! ब्रूस प्रिचर्ड, इसके पास कश्मीरी सीट हैं! अगर मैं WWE चैंपियन बन गया, तो क्या मैं लाल वाले जेट पर जा सकता हूँ? नहीं! स्टु, के-रिसीट्स का सीईओ! क्या तुम्हें वो तोहफा मिला? हैं? मुझे कोई तोहफा नहीं मिला। मैं तुम्हें कुछ देने वाला हूँ। क्या? वहाँ कुछ नहीं है, भाई। तुमने मुझे कभी कोई तोहफा नहीं भेजा। और ये सिर्फ तुम्हारे लिए नहीं था! ये और किसके लिए हो सकता है? तुम्हारी शादी का तोहफा? मैंने तुम्हें अंडरवियर दिया था। वही तो था। तुम्हारे लिए एक अंडरवियर। तुम्हारे लिए। शादी के तोहफे में तुम आम तौर पर यही देते हो, एक इंसान के लिए। यार, बस शांत हो जा। शांत हो जा। तुम्हारा बायाँ हाथ कहाँ है? तुम आपे से बाहर जा रहे हो, भाई। तुम्हे पता भी नहीं है। ओह। तुम आपे से बाहर जा रहे हो, यार। देवियों और सज्जनों, ये मेरा सौभाग्य है कि मैं आपको मैवरिक, लोगन से मिलवाऊँ। फैंस ने देखा कि लोगन ने सच में अपने प्रोमो के नोट्स अपनी हथेली पर लिखे हुए थे। भाई, वो मान गई। देखो देखो देखो। मुझे नहीं पता आप लोग किस बारे में बात कर रहे हो, ठीक है। मैंने प्रोमो मेरे हाथ पर नहीं लिखे हैं। ये, ये सब बकवास है। विज़न बराबर भविष्य। सीएम पंक कचरा है। ब्रॉक लेसनर। जेक पॉल एजे से बेहतर है। उन सबके लिए जो कहते हैं कि मैं सिर्फ WWE से लेता हूँ, आज शुक्रवार है, ठीक है? मैं प्यूर्टो रिको से डेनवर, कोलोराडो आया हूँ। अपने टीममेट को सपोर्ट करने के लिए आना, ये काफी दूर है। मैं अपना समय, अपनी मेहनत, अपनी एनर्जी दे रहा हूँ। ये सबूत है। आज रात, मैं अपने बंदे को मोरल सपोर्ट दे रहा हूँ। और अगर कुछ भी होता है, तो मैं बिग ब्रॉन्सन रीड के लिए वहाँ रहूँगा। द विज़न, बेबी। चलो। तू बेकार है! लोगन बेकार है! कार्मेलो को मौका मिला, दूसरी रस्सी से, फ्लाइंग क्लोथ्सलाइन, ब्रॉन्सन को बाहर किया। और ब्रॉन्सन रीड यहाँ बड़ी मुसीबत में है, कोरी। वो देखो! तुम ठीक हो, तुझे बस लॉक इन करना है। नाक से साँस लो, मुँह से बाहर निकालो ! वो सबकी धुलाई कर रहा है! ब्रॉड ब्रेकर! उसोज़ ने बीच में ही रोक दिया! लोगन पॉल जिमी से भिड़ गया, और अब लड़ाई शुरू हो गई है! एक और कारण कि हमने क्यों नहीं... नहीं, मैंने तो बस ऊँचाई की वजह से उनकी धुलाई कर दी। हाँ, हमने उनकी धुलाई कर दी। इसीलिए तुम्हारी साँस फूल रही है। हम अभी पहाड़ों में हैं। हम डेनवर, कोलोराडो में हैं, भाई। क्या, हम पाँच मील ऊपर हैं क्या? मुझे वो करके अच्छा लगा। तुम्हें सफर में दिक्कतें हुईं। मुझे सफर में दिक्कतें हुईं। हाँ, वैसे, मुझे असल में कोई दिक्कत नहीं हुई। हमारी नींद खराब हुई। मुझे असल में कोई दिक्कत नहीं हुई। मेरा जेट बहुत बड़ा था। यार, ये तो बिल्कुल अलग ही समस्या है। हाँ, उन सीटों पर बिठाने के लिए लोग ढूँढ़ना मुश्किल था। तुम उनके लिए पैसे दे रहे हो। वही तो समस्या थी। हम तीन लोग... क्या हमें इस बकवास की चिंता करनी पड़ती है? ये सच में पागलपन है। ठीक है, तो तुम देखोगे कि ये प्लेन उस प्लेन से अलग है जिस पर हम आज सुबह उड़े थे, खैर, ऐसा इसलिए है क्योंकि ये मेरे भाई का प्लेन है। भाई, बस ये कहना ही अच्छा लग रहा है। हमें आज रात प्यूर्टो रिको वापस उड़ना था, लेकिन हमारे प्लेन में खराबी आ गई और जेक वेगास में है। हम डेनवर, कोलोराडो में हैं। वो काफी पास हैं, उसने कहा भाई। मैं तुम्हें जेट भेजता हूँ, बस हमारे साथ F1 के लिए वेगास आ जाओ। तो मेरे भाई जेक पॉल ने सचमुच हम तीनों के लिए... ये जेट भेजा, और हम कल F1 के लिए वेगास में उससे मिलने जा रहे हैं। बहुत गज़ब, भाई। हे, भगवान! कैसे हो? बधाई हो! सुनो, टुडे शो पर हम याद हैं? हे, भगवान! तुम्हे वो याद है? मेरा पहला वायरल वाइन कंपाइलेशन बहुत चला था। मुझे न्यूयॉर्क में टुडे शो करने के लिए बुलाया गया था। तो, मैं कॉलेज में था, भाई, और मैं टुडे शो पर था। मैं गया, और मैं टेरी क्रूज़ से मिला। हम वाइन्स बनाते हैं, पर तुम उन पहले सेलिब्रिटीज़ में से एक हो जिनसे मैं कभी मिला था। मैं कभी नहीं भूलूँगा। मैं सोच रहा था कि ये लड़का। ये ठीक रहेगा। ये अच्छा लड़का है। और मैं ऐसा था, हे भगवान, लोगन पॉल। तुमने तो बस कब्ज़ा कर लिया। तुम जो कर रहे हो और तुमने ये सब जो हासिल किया, मुझे तुम पर बहुत गर्व है, यार। सुनो, तुम इस नई दुनिया को बहेतर तरीके से समझते हो। तुम समझ रहे हो न मैं क्या कह रहा हूँ? और यही बात मुझे पसंद है, यार। हम सबको बढ़ना है। हम सबको आगे बढ़ते रहना है। और तुम अभी भी शेप में हो। आह! मेरा एक बच्चा भी है। वाह! कैसे हो, लोगन? मिलकर अच्छा लगा। जो कर रहे हो, वो करते रहो। शुक्रिया, भाई। ओक्लाहोमा सिटी। ओक्लाहोमा सिटी। हवादार, धुंधला। सुंदर। बहुत ही खूबसूरत। ये तुमने मेरे लिए लिया? मैंने वो तुम्हारे लिए लिया। पर इस पर तो तुम्हारा नाम लिखा है। मुझे भेजा गया है तुम्हें देने के लिए। तुम्हें भेजा गया है मेरे लिए। क्या मैं इसे खोलूँ? ज़रूर। क्या ये लाल जेट पर एक सीट है? ये तुम्हारे लिए है। ओह, थैंक्स, ब्रूस। ये सच में मस्त है। पुरुषों और महिलाओं के लिए नोज़ वैक्स। बताओ, बंदर कैसी आवाज़ निकालता है? - Private Jet Billionaire Breaks Down Every Step in His Wealth Journey | The WSJ Money Interview: Kind: captions Language: en - I'm not waiting till I die. I wanna watch my kids enjoy their wealth. - When did you personally hit a billion dollars? - Probably... - Did it make you want to get rich? - No. - Really? He's a card-carrying member with a net worth above a billion dollars. As the number of ultra-wealthy people surges to a record high, Kenn is betting on increased demand. Today, we're gonna break down how he got here and reveal what spending in that tax bracket actually looks like. So let's go back to the beginning. I wanna hear how you made your first million. - So come out of the Air Force. I work for Northwest Orient for a brief period of time. I get furloughed. Unemployed, back living with my parents. And I don't know if you're familiar with shareholders roulette. When you have 50/50 owners, one person puts a number out there and the other person either gets to buy or sell at that number. So I had the obligation to present Raytheon with the number within 18 months of the closing. I had gotten the valuation underwritten by Warburg Pincus for $360 million. I offer Raytheon 180 million for their half of the business. And they thought I was stealing it and they bought me out. So I lose the whole thing in 2002. And it was the worst day of my life. But what's crazy about that was: at the time that that happened, I had already sold, I'd raised equity, and I really only owned about 16%. So when the proceeds came in, it was about $20 million. - Instead of catching up to NetJets, he was ousted from the company he founded. And in 2007 we sold it to Macquarie. And that was sold for $625 million. We had about 200 million of debt, so there was about 400 million of proceeds. So that was a big payday. That changed life significantly. - I wanna hear what you did to celebrate the Macquarie deal. - I know exactly what I did. I'm embarrassed to say. You know, they'll tell you that, when you have a big liquidity event, that you do nothing for six months. Just 'cause you're gonna do something stupid. And I did something stupid. So I went down and we took a two-week vacation, Mike and I and our wives, to Cabo San Lucas. And I bought a house, and I still have it. We still go down there and the families kind of spend a lot of time there. But, yeah, that was my celebration. - How much did you pay for the house? - Three and a half million. - Okay. And you had made hundreds of millions of dollars? - 80-ish. But Mike and I are partners too, so that wasn't all mine, right? - And you spent three million on the house. I mean, not bad. - I know, you're not supposed to do that. - You're not. Soon after, Ken clawed his way back to Flight Options. This time, he bought Raytheon out. Very important that they're not treated equally. Because life doesn't work that way. One's 12 years older than the other one. So one had 12 years of appreciation over the younger one. That doesn't mean we don't love you, it just mean, life, it's not gonna be equal. So don't like stand here like the three of you are equal. It's a lot of money for all of you. - Story of Kanika Tekriwal, The Founder of JetSetGo: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Are you ready to be inspired by the incredible story of a young woman, Empress, who refuses to let obstacles hold her back? Welcome to Startup Republic, your ultimate destination for inspiring success stories. Today we bring you the story of a young and dynamic woman from Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh, who has made her mark in the aviation industry. She is none other than Kanika Tekriwal. Also known as the Sky Queen of India, at just 33 years old, Kanika is the CEO of Jatt Set Co, India's first aircraft company. She has a net worth of 420 crores, but her path to success was easy. From battling cancer to facing financial struggles at the start of her business, Kanika has overcome incredible odds to achieve her dreams. So, sit back and prepare to be inspired by the Sky Queen's story of resilience, determination, and success. Kanika's story demonstrates the fact that When we dare to dream big, nothing can stop us from joining the ranks of high flyers. Her never give up attitude and unflinching determination to the success of aspiring [Music] Entrepreneurial Mindset Passion and Dedication Anything is Possible Growing up in a Marwari business family in Bhopal, Kanika learned business lessons early on in life. She graduated in Economics and Visual Communication from Mumbai and later pursued an MBA from Coventry University in London. Her experience in the aviation industry for over seven years and her acute observation led her to realize that people could afford to own a private jet, so she decided to provide the experience of a private jet to people who could not afford it. In 2014, she founded Jatt Set, a charter aircraft business similar to Ola and how ever. Starting her business was not easy as Kanika faced two significant challenges. A lack of money and a lack of customers never gave up. She overcame the hurdles and used advanced technology and smart management to increase air time, reduce ground time and make charter profitable. Today, Jatt Set Go has grown to a Rs 150 crore turnover company with around 200 employees and offices in Mumbai, Delhi, Chennai and Hyderabad. During 2020 to 2021, they handled 1 lakh flyers and operated 6000 flights. Kanika has received several awards and recognition for her outstanding achievements, including being named one of the BBC 100 Inspirational Women in the World and Phoebus Under 30 Asia, as one of the 30 Leading Entrepreneurs as a Young Global Leader by the World Economic Forum and received the National Entreprende Your Dreams We Hope Her success story has inspired you as much as it has Inspired Us Thank you for watching and don't forget to subscribe to our channel for more truly inspiring success stories
