- Work a Day on a Private Jet - Flight Attendant: Kind: captions Language: en Good morning and welcome to the airplane with me. I'm just getting all my stuff on board and I just want to walk through everything that I do to show you from beginning to end. Obviously, I can't film the time that I am dealing with the passengers, but I will be able to take the time to show you just how I do that without even anybody on board. So, let's get started. When you first get to the airplane, funny enough, the first thing that I want you to do is go flush the toilet. Well, good thing I flushed the toilet because there is no water cycling through that. So, I need to be able to tell the pilots right away, hey, I need the lab to be filled. And another reason that you want to check the laboratory right away is to be able to see, does it stink? If it smells, then you want to have them change out the fluid in the laboratory right away. Don't wait till the end. That's why we do it right at the beginning. when you first get on the airplane, I want you to become incredibly familiar with everything on it. If this is your very first time, now if it's not your first time, um even if it's your millionth time on the airplane, I want you to check all of your safety gear. Get real familiar with the safety equipment on your aircraft. Whether this is the first time you're working this airplane as a contractor or you're full-time, you do want to check the emergency equipment every single flight. Uh, don't assume just because you've worked on this airplane that you know where everything is. Be sure to check everything. Are the expiration dates um still further out? Are they still good? Does everything have power? Does everything have enough oxygen? Take everything out and inspect it. For your flashlights, make sure that they are on or that those there's going to be a little light blinking. For your life rafts, make sure that they are there, that they are secure. For the AED, pull it out and make sure that there is either a blinking light or something that says good. Every AED is going to be just a little bit different, but there will be a signal to say, "Hey, yes, this thing is good." Every plane's operating system will be just a little bit different. So, I want you to go directly to the panel and turn everything on. Some will be a digital panel and some will be a simple panel with buttons. When you're getting your aircraft ready and taking delivery of all the catering, things get a little bit messy before they get really neat. So, here I sped up the video just so that you can see. I'm taking all the catering from the boxes and putting it in a cooler. I don't have a chiller, so I put everything in a cooler with some ice on it. If your aircraft has a chiller, put everything in there as well to keep the food cold. Next, get ice from the line personnel and fill up all of your ice drawers. You're going to want to cover all your drinks with ice. And then as well, have a drawer designated for just clean ice. This means that you're not going to put be putting any cans in there or any other thing. It's just going to be clean ice to be able to use to scoop for drinks. So, here you can see I have one clean ice drawer and one another drawer with drinks in it and a second drawer with drinks as well. Keep them all covered. If your aircraft does not have a coffee maker, you can fill up coffee pots, one with regular, one with decaf. My aircraft also has an espresso maker, which I depend on that thing heavily. For coffee cups, you can either use a ceramic mug or I prefer to use paper cups with lids. I know it's not the fanciest thing, but if you were to have turbulence, nothing spills. Next, since I do not have a chiller, I also use my sink as another area to keep things cold. So, I will fill up the sink with another bag of ice. And then I'll take tall things like milk, juice, cream, things like that that won't fit in one of my ice drawers. I put them in here. It's just another great way to keep things cold. Be careful when you're pulling out the trash bags from the bins. These fire prongs in the back will rip the bag. So, be careful of that. Those fire prongs are meant to go off and put in a fire retardant should there be a fire in there at all. Don't overfill the bag if you can um because that'll just make it harder for you to pull the bag out. I usually set up the fruit bowl while I'm in the galley, but I have a little bit more space here on the credenza just to show you how I set everything up. All right, so I have my fruit bowl. You can have a tall bowl like this, a more shallow bowl. One thing I do like to do is make sure that I have something uh like non non-skid underneath it so it doesn't slide. Now, this will stay for takeoff. Sometimes for landing, especially if I know we're going to have like an abrupt stop, I will then remove this and put this in the galley for landing. But most of the time, this will stay here. So, I like to start with some bananas. Maybe three, four bananas. Always make sure that you're taking any of the little stickies off. I'll start with bananas in here. Okay. Make sure these come off. I'll stack my apples in. Next, I have some cutie oranges. I'll stack those in as well. And then I've already pre-washed my grapes. I have some clusters. Some people like grapes, some people don't. Um my people on the airplane always love grapes. These always get eaten. So I'll just kind of spread those out in there. And then I like to leave napkins next to it so that you know when people are eating the oranges, eating the apples, I have something directly to put um their peels or their rind in. So that is what my fruit tray will look like. Now next, my snack baskets. A lot of flight attendants will not refill snack baskets after they've worked on the plane, which honestly kind of irritates me. I always try to refill them for other people and then before I get off my own plane, I will make sure that I have refilled the snack basket. So, I'll just show you what I have. I have some beef jerky. I've got some um healthy snacks like dried fruit. I've got all the junk food. I do try to make sure that everything is turned directly out. And then if I need more, check in the drawers. Then I'll pull things out from here, put them out if needed. I just try to make sure that everything in all the snack baskets uh looks full. You don't want somebody to get to the snack basket and be like, "Oh, well, this thing is quite picked over." Also, it's good to put some napkins in with the snack basket as well for people to see. And I have two snack baskets. So I will get that one filled as well. One little touch that I like to do at every seat is put a water bottle right away before the guests get there. And then I include a couple mints and Starburst right in the cup holder next to them if there's two cup holders in a row. And I find that it's just a sweet little treat. And a lot of people really do eat these. So, it's just a little touch. You don't have to do it, but it's something that I like to do. [Music] So, your passengers are now on the airplane. Now, what I want you to go through right away and ask everybody, "What can I get for you to drink?" Oftentimes, I will keep water or if I know their drink of choice, I will keep it next to their seat. And then if they don't want water, if they want something more than that, then I'll go through right away, serve all the drinks that I possibly can before takeoff. Now, one thing that I will not serve before takeoff is wine. usually because it's sitting in a much taller glass. Or if I do, then I will tell them, hey, be sure to hang on to this for takeoff or you will be wearing this in your lap. And that usually gets people to think, okay, yes, I need to remember to hang on to that. And if you have time, go ahead and set up things on your credenza as a welcome table. But remember, you might have a short uh taxi time, so you're going to need to get everything cleaned up off that credenza before you take off. Sometimes, you know, you might have less than five minutes to do drinks and get everything out and then put away or like if you're sitting somewhere like Teterboro, New York or Van Eyes, you could be number 20 in line. So, you're going to have you're going to have to gauge for yourself what kind of time that you're going to have in order to get, you know, drinks and any kind of pre-ervice done. But always remember, you can ask your pilots as well, hey, how long am I going to have uh in order to serve people before we take off? Now, I would not serve any type of meals, but if there's any kind of, you know, warm nuts, little tiny light bites, that would be okay. But keep any type of appetizers or meals until after you take off. So, make sure that you get everything cleaned up and put away for takeoff. Time to buckle up. Now, every jump seat in every airplane is going to be just a little bit different. So, get familiar with how the jump seat works, how the harness works, and the seat belt, as well as the seat belt release. All right. So, now you've taken off, you've passed 10,000 ft, and the pilots give you the okay to stand up. What comes next? Well, I would go through the cabin and again ask if you can get anything to drink or give any kind of refills. I will often after that start with hot towel service or if it's really hot out, then I'll do a cold towel service. Then I'll ask if everybody is interested in eating and I'll tell them what I have on the menu for them or remind them of what it is that they had specifically ordered beforehand. All right, now that my passengers are all off the airplane, I'm going to go through everything and make sure that they have not left anything behind. So, I want you checking behind all things. Pull seats out. Check behind things. Those are mine. Did anybody leave? Stuff in here. So basically, you just want to check all the nooks and crannies. Make sure that nobody left anything underneath seats, behind seats, tucked in anywhere. And then don't forget to check check the baggage compartment. I know the pilots are usually the ones who empty that, but make sure that you haven't left any coats hanging up or anything else, small bags, that kind of thing. So I like to use a mix of alcohol and water and a microfiber and wipe everything down. So spray down the seats and anywhere in the nooks and crannies of the leather. Be sure that you're checking in there and wiping things out Then I want you to pull out every table surface. Every table surface. And even if you don't think it got used, be sure to wipe them down. And then I want you to wipe down all the windows as well. Nothing tackier than having a bunch of fingerprints. Now, before you get started, you know, when you're loading everything on the airplane, one thing I would suggest is to wipe down all your surfaces before passengers get there, especially if you were not the last one on the aircraft. You never know what got left prior. So, it's really good to just go through and check all the surfaces beforehand. And then always make sure to wipe things down afterwards. Even if you think you are, you know, going to be the one working the plane um the next day, just be sure that you are leaving the aircraft absolutely spotless cuz you never know, things could change and you could be taken off a trip or another flight attendant's going to be coming in. So, you do not want to leave any mess, whether that is for yourself or for another flight attendant the next day. For most of the airplane, I do like to use the microfiber cloth. But when it comes to cleaning the laboratory, I don't want you to use that microfiber cloth. I want you to use something disposable so that after you wipe down everything in the laboratory, including the sink and the toilet, that you can throw this out. I don't want you to use the microfiber cloth to, you know, wipe down the whole airplane, then the lavatortory, and think, "Oh, I just need to wipe something else down." And you grab that microfiber that you just wiped the toilet with, and you wipe something else. So, put your gloves on, grab some paper towel and your spray, and then get going in the bathroom. And be sure to throw all of that out before you touch anything else. Now, for the mirror, I will grab a brand new paper towel and wipe all of that down separately from the paper towel that I use to clean the toilet. Fold the ends of the toilet paper in a nice little triangle. And there's other ways to get even fancier with this, but this is just a basic fold to make things look nice. Then be sure to empty the trash either midway through flight if it gets too full or especially at the end, always leave a fresh trash bag for the next flight. All right, now that you have the airplane entirely clean, there's going to be items that you are going to give to the line personnel. Now, sometimes you can get these to them right away and then get the rest of the airplane clean or clean it all up and then hand them over, depending on whatever works for you. So today I didn't have too many linens. I just have um some napkins. So I put it in a laundry bag and I'm going to hand this over. I usually do label this um before I hand it over so they know remember what airplane this goes to. And then also I have my dish bucket. I'm going to hand this down to them. It is labeled as well. And then I'll just tell them, hey, this is what day uh we're headed back out. most times um know they can get dishes done within a couple hours. As far as laundry goes, I do like to wait until I either make it back to my home base or if I know that I'm going to be somewhere for more than 24 hours cuz sometimes laundry uh it gets dry cleananed and it'll often take much longer. So, I don't want you to get caught without your laundry if it's a quick turn. Um you know, hang on to this. But you use your judgment as far as how long you're going to hang on to the laundry. I also keep a much larger bag for like blankets. So, after every charter, I do take the blankets off and I have those dry cleaned. I do have blankets um that are specifically for charter flights and I have other blankets that are specifically for the owners and I do keep those separate so that the owner stuff stays much nicer. But that's what I am handing. That is what I hand over to the line guys and then hand over your trash as well. One thing that you can hand over to the line guys also or line personnel is if you have a cooler bag, fill that up with all your leftovers that you want to bring back onto the airplane. Maybe your your milk, your juice, your lemons, limes, that kind of thing. Hand them a cooler. Have that labeled as well.

- Vaunt Membership Review 2025 | Pros and Cons – Honest & Unbiased: Kind: captions Language: en I've been exploring the Vaunt membership for a while now and this video is my honest and unbiased review on the same. I'll cover all the main points from key features to risks and considerations. So, by the end of this video, you'll have a better understanding of the VA membership. First, let's look at what the VA membership offers. Vaunt is a private jet membership that provides access to lastminute one-way flights across the country for a flat annual fee for $1,995 per year. Members can join weight lists for available flights and if selected fly with up to four passengers at no additional cost. There are no perflight fees, hidden costs, or baggage fees. Once you're selected for a flight, a concierge team assists with all the details, ensuring a seamless travel experience. So, what's good about it? The concept of flying private without the hefty price tag is appealing. The ability to access lastminute flights and have the entire jet to yourself offers a level of convenience and luxury that's hard to beat. The process is straightforward. Download the app, join weight lists for flights, and if you're selected, confirm your booking. The concier service adds a personal touch, handling all the arrangements for you. However, there are some considerations to keep in mind. The biggest challenge is the weight list system. Flights are not guaranteed. You can join as many weight lists as you like, but there's no certainty you'll be selected. Some members have reported being on multiple weight lists without securing a flight. Additionally, flights are one way, so you'll need to arrange your return travel separately. The app's user interface has received mixed reviews with some users finding it less intuitive than expected. Now, let's talk about the user experience. The app is available for download on both iOS and Android devices. Once installed, you can browse available flights, join weight lists, and track your status. If selected, you'll receive a notification 24 hours before departure, and the concierge team will reach out to finalize the details. The flights themselves are operated by a fleet of luxury jets, including the Honda Jet Elite and Citation CG3, ensuring a comfortable and private journey. Overall, the VA membership is an innovative approach to private jet travel, offering a cost-effective way to experience luxury flying. However, the reliance on the weightless system and the uncertainty of flight availability may not suit everyone. If you have flexible travel plans and enjoy the thrill of spontaneous trips, VA could be a great fit. But if you require guaranteed flights on specific dates, this model might not meet your needs. That's all for this video. I hope you found this video helpful. And if you did, make sure you give this video a super thanks by clicking on the heart icon to help support the channel. - Comparing XO vs NetJets - 2025 Review: Kind: captions Language: en Hey everyone. Today we're comparing two big names in private jet travel. Exo and NetJet. Let's break them down and see which one fits your needs best. First, Exo. It's a flexible ondemand private jet service. You can book a whole jet, a single seat, or even crowdsource a flight to save money. Exo has access to over 2,000 planes worldwide. From small turbo props to big VIP jets, they offer memberships starting at $595 a year with higher tiers for priority booking. You can book instantly online or through their app and they focus on a smooth modern experience with perks like Wi-Fi and concierge services. Now, NetJets, it's the world's largest private jet company owned by Birkshshire Hathaway. They specialize in fractional ownership where you buy a share of a jet or jet card programs for prepaid hours. Their fleet has over 800 planes, mostly high-end jets like the Bombardier Global. NetJets is known for top safety and standards, consistent service, and the premier feel, but it requires a bigger upfront investment. So, how do they compare? Exo is great for flexibility. If you fly occasionally or want to book last minute, XO's pay as you go model, and shares flights are budget friendly. NetJets, though, is better for frequent flyers who want a guaranteed jet and consistent luxury. It's pricier, but you get reliability and a larger fleet. Who wins? If you're a casual flyer or want cheaper options, go with XO. If you fly a lot and value ownership or premium service, NetJets is your pick. It depends on your travel style. Thanks for watching. Drop a comment with your thoughts and like and subscribe for more.

- Spruce Creek - The Town Built For Private Planes: Kind: captions Language: en Today's video I would like to introduce to&nbsp; everyone the most unique town in the world&nbsp;&nbsp; where people fly just to go to work or to&nbsp; have breakfast every day it is considered&nbsp;&nbsp; a Dreamland for those who love the sky Spruce&nbsp; Creek is one of the most unique residential&nbsp;&nbsp; communities in the world where has residents&nbsp; almost each of whom own a plane known as a&nbsp;&nbsp; residential air park or a Flyin community in&nbsp; Northeast Florida a few miles south of Daytona Beach Spruce Creek is home to about 1,300&nbsp; homes and 700 hangers The Hanger homes come&nbsp;&nbsp; with direct access to the taxiways some of the&nbsp; homes here also have observation towers where&nbsp;&nbsp; you can watch planes fly by instead of a&nbsp; garage most houses in Spruce Creek have&nbsp;&nbsp; attached hanger and the driveway leads directly&nbsp; to a 4,000 by 150 ft Runway with GPS approach&nbsp;&nbsp; there is an 18-hole Golf Course several flying&nbsp; clubs rental aircraft and Flight Training and&nbsp;&nbsp; 24-hour patrolled security for those whose lives&nbsp; revolve around airplanes Spruce Creek is a [Music] paradise why is that you have to spend hours&nbsp; preparing to pack move to the airport then&nbsp;&nbsp; continue to wait a few hours to line up get&nbsp; tickets check in here you just need to open&nbsp;&nbsp; the garage door and start rolling&nbsp; wheel on the runway and then take off but Spruce Creek isn't the only residential&nbsp; air Park in the country the concept first&nbsp;&nbsp; developed after World War II a time period when&nbsp; the United States had an incredible surplus of&nbsp;&nbsp; both airfields and Pilots created by the war whose&nbsp; population had ballooned from fewer than 34,000 in&nbsp;&nbsp; 193 39 to more than 400,000 by 1946 in order to&nbsp; put countless deactivated military strips Across&nbsp;&nbsp; the Nation to good use and to accommodate the&nbsp; burgeoning pilot population the Civil Aeronautics&nbsp;&nbsp; Administration proposed the construction of 6,000&nbsp; residential air Parks throughout the country while&nbsp;&nbsp; that number was never fulfilled the initial&nbsp; proposal generated enough momentum to pave the&nbsp;&nbsp; way for decades worth of interest and investment&nbsp; in what has become a large and Active network of&nbsp;&nbsp; Flyin communities the Navy built Spruce Creek and&nbsp; added an airport in late 1943 used as a training&nbsp;&nbsp; facility it was no longer needed at the end of the&nbsp; war sold to the city of Daytona Beach in 1957 the&nbsp;&nbsp; location was relatively unused until Navy ensen&nbsp; McKinley Conway came into the picture Conway came&nbsp;&nbsp; up with the idea of a residential neighborhood&nbsp; where you could taxi your plane right from your&nbsp;&nbsp; door the city of Daytona Beach told Conway he&nbsp; could purchase the property which is when Conway&nbsp;&nbsp; assembled a group of investors for the project in&nbsp; July 1970 the site was approved when investor Jay&nbsp;&nbsp; Thompson acquired the Spruce Creek airport in&nbsp; the late 1970s he focused on turning the space&nbsp;&nbsp; into a country club like space with a clubhouse&nbsp; tennis courts a restaurant and an 18 whole Golf Course according to Bravo who has lived here&nbsp; for 20 years there's no Flyin Community with&nbsp;&nbsp; the size and appeal of Spruce Creek he has&nbsp; been a pilot since the age of 16 and spent&nbsp;&nbsp; 6 months searching for the right Flyin&nbsp; houses and brick houses but this one is truly luxurious the people of Spruce Creek live&nbsp; in a tightly knit Community most of them&nbsp;&nbsp; are professional pilots and they talk&nbsp; in aviation jargon others are doctors&nbsp;&nbsp; lawyers and land speculators but all of&nbsp; them are without exception nuts about Aviation every Saturday morning some of them&nbsp; would gather beside the runway take off in&nbsp;&nbsp; groups of three and fly to one of the local&nbsp; airports for breakfast a tradition they call&nbsp;&nbsp; the Saturday morning [Music] Gaggle people&nbsp; living in Spruce Creek all have high income&nbsp;&nbsp; levels how houses at Spruce Creek are priced&nbsp; from $169,000 250,000 USD about 4 billion to&nbsp;&nbsp; 60 billion vnd depending on the area meanwhile&nbsp; larger villas with airplane hangers cost up to&nbsp;&nbsp; millions of dollars in addition to a high standard&nbsp; serve entertainment needs in addition this place&nbsp;&nbsp; also has many small lakes containing all kinds of&nbsp; wild animals Pilots are constantly worried about&nbsp;&nbsp; birds including vultures Gibbons and Eagles&nbsp; was to take us out over the North Carolina coast and then the Atlantic Ocean now on a commercial flight you'd have to wait for an update from the pilot in a situation like this but on board a Gulfstream just walk into the cockpit and ask what's going on our flight plan has just changed we're having to go along the coast instead of straight out over the Atlantic Ocean because there's a rocket launch and so there's a temporary flight restriction that's adjusted the flight the airspace that we can operate in I won't complain about more time up here but thankfully the pilots decided to take on more fuel than we needed back in Greensboro so it's less expensive so we've got the fuel we need we'll just keep on trucking onboard service began with a hot towel now this was a morning - The Real Cost Of Owning A Private Jet: Kind: captions Language: en from The Upfront price to maintenance Fuel and even parking fees owning a private jet comes with jawdropping expenses let's break it all down and see how stars like Lionel Messi Taylor Swift and Bill Gates manage their Skyhigh Investments private jets are the ultimate status symbol but most people don't realize how much it costs beyond the initial purchase it's not just about buying the jet J it's the ongoing expenses that add up to understand what we're dealing with let's start with the purchase price the initial purchase the initial cost of purchasing a private jet varies significantly based on the size model and amenities included smaller Jets like the Cessna Citation M2 designed for short Regional flights can cost around $5 million making them accessible for wealthy individuals who prioritize efficiency over luxury on the other end of the spectrum top tier models like the Gulf Stream g700 known for its long- range capabilities and Ultra comfortable Interiors can reach prices over $75 million for celebrities jet ownership is often a statement of luxury and convenience Lionel Messi for example bought an Embraer Legacy 650 for around $25 million offering ample space and comfort for his travels Taylor Swift with her massive touring schedule opted for a daol falcon 900 costing about $40 million providing her with a reliable and stylish jet meanwhile Bill Gates chose the bombarder global Express a sophisticated aircraft worth approximately $70 million reflecting his preference for cuttingedge technology and long-distance capabilities depreciation owning a private jet is much like owning a luxury car in terms of depreciation but on a much larger scale within the first 5 years a jet can lose 30% or more of its initial value this rapid depreciation is partly due to advances in aviation technology where newer models with better efficiency and amenities are constantly introduced to the market for example if someone buys a jet for $50 million it could be worth as little as $35 million after just 5 years celebrities like Messi who owns the Embraer Legacy 650 experience this loss of value firsthand especially when market demand shifts toward newer aircraft models however certain Jets retain their value better due to their popularity or limited production the more frequently a jet is used the faster it depreciates as wear and tear also contribute to its reduced market value despite this the Rich and Famous often have the financial flexibility to absorb these losses maintenance costs maintaining a private jet is a significant ongoing expense that owners must factor in when considering the true cost of ownership unlike commercial aircraft which have dedicated ated ground Crews private jets require regular maintenance to ensure their safety and functionality for most Jets this translates into annual maintenance costs ranging from $500,000 to $1 million depending on the aircraft size and complexity regular maintenance includes engine checks inspections of avionic systems and structural evaluations to ensure the jet is flight ready in addition unforeseen repairs such as engine failures damage to the fuselage or issues with landing gear can add another one to2 million every few years for example Messi's Embraer Legacy 650 undergos detailed inspections annually with significant costs for replacing parts or upgrading systems larger Jets like Taylor Swift's desol Falcon 900 often require more frequent and costly maintenance due to their advanced technology keeping a jet in top condition is essential for both safety and performance making maintenance one of the biggest ongoing expenses fuel costs fuel costs are among the largest recurring expenses for private jet owners and they can quickly add up especially for frequent flyers on average jet fuel prices range between $4 and $5 per gallon and depending on the size of the aircraft a jet can burn anywhere from 200 to 500 gallons of fuel per hour for instance a typical transatlantic flight on a private jet can cost up to $50,000 INF fuel alone making Long Haul trips particularly expensive a larger jet like Bill Gates's bombarder Global Express consumes significantly more fuel than smaller aircraft pushing the costs even higher while smaller Jets like Messi's Embraer Legacy 650 are more fuel efficient the cost Still Remains substantial especially with frequent long-distance travel furthermore fuel prices fluctuate which can make it difficult for owners to predict costs accurately regardless of the jet size fuel consumption is one of the most consistent and expensive aspects of private jet ownership crew salaries to operate a private jet you need a skilled crew and that comes at a steep cost a typical crew includes two pilots since Federal Regulations require two for safety during long flights as well as at least one flight attendant to ensure passenger Comfort pilot salaries alone range from $150,000 to $200,000 per year with long Hall Pilots often earning more due to the extra hours and complex flight planning required for international travel flight attendants who ensure safety and provide personalized Services during flights typically earn between $5,000 and $70,000 annually for larger Jets or jets with more frequent travel this cost can escalate with some owners paying upwards of $500,000 per year just for Staffing celebrities like Messi and Swift who often travel internationally for events or tours often employ highly trained experienced Crews to ensure their safety and comfort hanger fees and parking when not in use a private jet needs a place to stay and hanger fees are a crucial part of jet ownership costs renting space at an airport to park a private jet can cost anywhere from $50,000 to $200,000 annually depending on the location airport size and the type of aircraft insurance insurance for a private jet is essential and often surprisingly costly coverage typically includes whole Insurance to protect the aircraft itself liability insurance for damages caused during flight and additional coverage for passengers and crew for a private jet worth $50 million insurance premiums can range from $500,000 annually depending on the aircraft's value usage and the owner's risk profile additional luxuries one of the main reasons people purchase private jets is the ability to customize them with luxury features that reflect their personal style the cost of these upgrades can be astronomical with custom interiors entertainment systems and other luxurious touches pushing the price of a jet well beyond its base price while celebrities like Messi Swift and Gates can afford it even they face significant expenses to keep their jets in the air for most people fractional ownership or jet Charters might be the smarter choice but for the ultra wealthy the sky is truly the limit if you found this breakdown eye opening hit the like button and subscribe for more deep dives into the cost of luxury what's your dream private jet let us know in the comments

- How much does it cost to fly private? - Rachel Yang: Kind: captions Language: en Across 2023 and 2024, one internationally renowned musician flew their private jet 396 times in 365 days. And one of those trips was just six minutes long. Figures like this aren’t uncommon among the world’s richest jet-setters— in fact, this frequent flyer probably isn’t even the celebrity you’re thinking of. So what's the price tag for this high flying lifestyle? The majority of private flying happens in the US, where even a relatively short trip is incredibly expensive. If you want to fly private, you have two options: buying a jet or renting one. Jet rentals can run from roughly $7,500 an hour for domestic flights to roughly $17,000 an hour for international ones. For context, that’s over $40,000 to fly from LA to New York, and over $120,000 to fly from New York to London. And that's before paying an additional several thousand dollars for the crew, the fuel, landing fees for the airport, and federal taxes. That said, these rental costs are still much cheaper than actually buying a plane. Depending on their size, new jets run anywhere from $2 to $65 million. Then, in addition to the usual operational costs for each flight, jet owners still have to pay for a hangar to park in and up to $1 million a year for routine inspections and maintenance. This is already absurdly expensive. But private flying also has a major environmental cost. While both private and commercial planes emit a lot of CO2, commercial planes do so to move a lot of people. For example, in 2022, commercial airplanes emitted roughly 800 megatons of CO2 moving about 853 million passengers. That’s a little less than one ton of CO2 per person per flight. On average, most people emit four tons of CO2 a year— so taking two round trip flights would double their emissions. This math already isn’t great, but private jets make it so much worse. With only a handful of people onboard, private flights emit 10 to 20 times more emissions per passenger on average. And since their passengers tend to be frequent flyers, their individual emissions are astronomical. In 2023 alone, the top ten US private fliers produced more combined emissions than 6,500 people. And for the past few years, roughly 1% of fliers have been responsible for almost half of aviation emissions. This discrepancy is huge, and it will likely continue growing as the private aviation industry expands. However, while we probably can’t convince celebrities and CEOs to fly commercial, one promising approach for reducing plane emissions is swapping existing jet fuel for Sustainable Aviation Fuels, or SAF. Traditional jet fuel is made from fossil fuels and releases carbon into the atmosphere when burned. SAFs, on the other hand, are made from waste materials like used cooking oil, and renewable crops like corn and algae. These biomaterials still emit carbon when burned, but growing these crops pulls enough CO2 out of the atmosphere to offset those emissions by up to 86%. If the entire jet fuel pipeline shifted to SAFs, it could reduce aviation emissions by up to 94%. But for this to happen, SAF producers need to overcome two obstacles. First, there aren't enough crops to produce biofuels on a large scale. Researchers are developing synthetic SAFs that don’t require crops, but they’re still being rolled out. Second, current SAF production is relatively small scale, which makes it extremely expensive. At three times the cost of normal fuel, most airlines won’t even consider making the switch, and if they did it would cause airfare to skyrocket. Governments in Singapore, Japan, and most notably the EU have already started programs to support widespread SAF adoption and fund their development. But this is where private fliers could actually help the environment. Most jet owners already spend hundreds of thousands of dollars a year on private flights, and it would only cost them a few thousand more per flight to use synthetic SAFs. This boom in usage would fund both SAF development and large-scale production. And those advances would make sustainable fuel cheaper for everyone— both the people flying private and those privately wishing they were.

with only a few trips that extend beyond 2000 miles in this case it would be better if you opted for a lower range aircraft longer trips can be accommodated by stopovers or by renting a longer range aircraft when needed you don't want to waste money on a larger aircraft that you would only use twice a year for longer trips one of the most important expenditures when it comes to operating an conditions for your aircraft will rest entirely on the crew's abilities as a result it's critical to ensure that your crew members are properly qualified and skilled the higher the crew's expertise level however the higher the wage they expect as of may 2022 private jet flight attendants in the united states make on average forty six thousand ninety six dollars per year in terms of salary this amounts to about twenty two dollars and sixteen cents per hour eight hundred and eighty six dollars per week or three thousand eight hundred and forty one dollars per month private jet pilots on the other hand make an average of ninety three thousand three hundred and ninety five dollars per year again it is important to keep in mind that different jets require different certifications from pilot crews the salaries of the flight crew as a whole are determined in part by their experience and qualifications private jet owners have the option to hire contract flight crews or make their flight crew full-time employees the latter while significantly costlier remains the preferred option for jet owners who travel often and regularly if you plan on only making 10 trips per year hiring a full-time crew is not the best option if you are thinking about owning a private jet it is important to remember that your aircraft will spend the majority of its time on the ground and not in the air whether you place it in a hanger or tie down can have drastic consequences on its resale value and its overall lifespan and can greatly affect the cost of maintaining the aircraft let's go over the advantages and drawbacks of both if done correctly tying down your aircraft may be a cost-effective and totally reasonable way to park at an airport with options usually ranging from 50 to 150 per month the three-point tie-down is the most effective method for doing so and it entails firmly securing your aircraft's wings and tail this will protect your plane from tipping due to jet prop exhaust or heavy winds if done correctly keeping your plane tethered rather than hangered might save you thousands of dollars each year but it may end up costing you more in the long term owing to repairs and upkeep in harsh conditions tie downs are the cheapest option but leaving a very expensive aircraft at the mercy of the elements is arguably not the wisest solution hangers on the other hand are considered the gold standard of aircraft storage hanger storage is significantly costlier than using a tie down costing up to 500 to 600 per month many would consider this option much preferable to tie downs despite the steep difference in price business and individuals that own and operate airplanes face risks that are unique accidents can result in significant injuries or even death as well as damage or destruction to the aircraft and or other property as a result in the eyes of the law aircraft insurance coverage is critical there are two main kinds of insurance that you as a private jet owner have to take into consideration liability insurance and hole insurance let's go over both of these and see why they are so important liability insurance covers three types of liability bodily injury or death sustained by third parties other than passengers bodily injury or deaths sustained by passengers and damage to property owned by third parties these coverages can be acquired separately with each type of coverage having its own occurrence limit alternatively all three might be covered by a single agreement with a smooth limit or a single combined limit per occurrence some dangers may not be covered by aircraft liability insurance the following are examples of common exclusions expected or intended injury bodily injury to employees liability imposed under a worker's compensation law contractual liability injury or damage caused by the application of fertilizers or other substances relating to crop dusting injury or damage caused by pollution noise or electrical or electromagnetic interference hole insurance on the other hand is meant to protect businesses and individuals that own aircraft from physical damage to said aircraft hole insurance has three main options for coverage ground and flight is a category that covers damages to the aircraft that are dealt through peril which in this case includes the disappearance of the aircraft this category includes damages occurring while the plane is in the air as well as on the ground not in flight is a category that covers damages taking place exclusively while the aircraft is not in flight whether it be moving or stationary finally the last category is not in motion and it only covers damages to the aircraft while it is stationary it is important to keep in mind that hole insurance does not cover any damages to the aircraft that are covered by wear and tear furthermore it also excludes damages caused by electrical breakdown barrels related to terrorist acts or warfare hijacking overheating of the engines or government confiscation overall it is important to keep in mind that claims to damages are made on the basis of the agreed value of the plane which is determined per contract between you and the insurer the insurance will pay the agreed value if an aircraft is deemed a total loss if the plane is only partially damaged the amount paid by the insurer is determined by who executes the repairs if the repairs are done by a tertiary entity such as an aviation repair facility the insurance usually covers the cost of repairing or replacing the damaged property as well as the cost of transportation to and from the repair shop if the insurer does the repairs the insurance covers supplies labor overhead calculated as a percentage of labor expenses and transportation the cost of transporting the plane to and from the repair location private jet management is a popular but optional feature of private jet ownership aircraft management encompasses all of the responsibilities and services provided by an aircraft management firm opting for this option allows your aircraft to be looked after and scheduled to a private jet management firm these company services are geared to ensure that your plane has pilots is properly maintained and that your travels are organized there are basically two types of private jet management services available charter aircraft management and turnkey aircraft management if you the aircraft owner hire a business to lease out your aircraft when it is not in use this is known as charter aircraft management this can be used by aircraft owners to reduce the yearly cost of owning and maintaining the aircraft a charter aircraft management firm will make arrangements for the necessary charter certificates as well as take care of scheduling turnkey aircraft management is a more comprehensive management package this type of management takes charge of all day-to-day operations related to the aircraft the aircraft management firm is in charge of maintenance repairs crew employment training security and certification a turnkey private jet management business can usually arrange for your aircraft to be chartered as well as a result even if you want to charge your plane you'll just have to deal with one provider it remains the case however that many aircraft owners opt for managing their aircraft themselves this option entails leaving the management of the aircraft to the flight crew rather than a third party this option is useful when you don't fly a lot if the crew is only needed for example for 400 hours a year then they will have the ability to manage all aspects of aircraft management so long as these responsibilities are reflected in their paychecks all in all this option can end up saving you a lot of money if you do not intend to fly a lot during the year jet aircraft do not use the same fuel as smaller piston-powered planes they instead use jet fuel this allows jets to be provided with a greater power and efficiency when compared to gasoline for example because gasoline has a significantly lower flash point than jet fuel furthermore it also allows the jet to fly at much higher altitudes than piston aircraft some jets fly as high as 50 thousand feet at these altitudes gasoline freezes due to the lower air temperatures while jet fuel is able to remain liquid due to its lower freezing point furthermore jet fuel is also significantly cheaper than gasoline when flying a private jet fuel costs are a considerable hourly expense the longer you fly the more gasoline the plane consumes some planes consume much more fuel than others and so this cost will vary depending on your aircraft the distance you fly each year as well as where you procure the fuel in question for example opting to have a turnkey jet management company manage your aircraft sometimes allows you to buy fuel at a significant discount maintenance is required at regular intervals as a means of ensuring that airplanes are safe to operate these intervals are predetermined and are based on the manufacturer's recommendations furthermore maintenance is planned according to the number of hours flown a certain aircraft for example may require an inspection at an interval of 100 hours of flying all in all the more the plane flies the more maintenance it will require while this type of cost is regular and seems easy to quantify it is important to keep in mind that it would include any unforeseen events that require further maintenance or even repairs to the aircraft these include events such as birch strikes and blown tires that will often prompt a maintenance check as soon as possible to return the aircraft's airworthiness engine overhauls are a crucial part of maintaining an aircraft's engine these operations intel inspecting and repairing parts of the engine that need maintenance the process however is a little bit more expensive than a simple oil check on a honda civic jet aircraft also require an even more expensive checkup called a major engine overhaul which entails the entire disassembling and reassembling of the engine in question today's business jets have an average yearly flying duration of 350 to 500 hours based on that premise a new aircraft with a 4 000 hour time between overhaul cycle or tbo cycle might realistically expect 8 to 11 years of flying time before the first major overhaul is required you the owner or the entity in charge of maintaining the aircraft will have to find and select an engine overall partner who would be willing and capable of taking care of your specific engine this partner will then coordinate with you or your representative the elements entailed by the overhaul which include the removal and reinstallation of the engine it's shipping to a specific location as well as the running of test runs and acceptance flights it is very important to make sure the partner is completely able to coordinate each step of the project still in the spirit of preparation it might be a good idea to look for temporary replacement options before sending your engines for overhaul here you can either opt for leasing temporary replacement engines or instead grounding the aircraft and leasing replacement jet [Music] these costs will vary wildly depending on the distance and routes flown crew fees are fees paid to the crew of the aircraft while there is an extended stay at a specific location they will vary based on the location and length of stay as well as on the basis of the pilot's employment contract the location of the stay is of crucial importance because some locations are significantly more expensive to stay in than others a night in los angeles california will be significantly more expensive than a night in omaha nebraska landing fees are charged by the airport and on the basis of the aircraft's weight class handling fees are also important as they entail the airport service in terms of parking unloading the aircraft and so on as such these fees will vary in function of the number of trips as well as the airports visited as different airports charge different landing fees again as an example the lax airport will charge significantly higher landing fees than a smaller less frequented airport such as the neighboring van nuys airport this airport because of its relatively smaller size compared to lax cannot accommodate large commercial flights and is instead geared towards smaller aircraft such as private jets and will thus generally offer better value for these smaller aircraft well guys that's it for this video click the links above to check out some of our other videos also don't forget to like and subscribe for more content like this we have many great videos coming up see you in the next one

It also has a very strategic layout with a surgical suite and two kitchens capable of preparing up to 100 meals at a time. It can even be refueled midair given the plane's unlimited range. >> Does Trump say, "I don't care about these systems. I just like the interior. Do we cut corners and fly around in a less capable, less safe Air Force One?" Here in the US, the rise of private flying is closely tied with the history of Air Force One. Franklin Roosevelt became the first president to fly in a private purpose-built plane in 1945 when he traveled to Crimea during World War II. Roosevelt's plane had a conference room, a special wheelchair lift, a bulletproof window, and a refrigerator, the epitome of luxury at the time. In 1953, President Dwight Eisenhower became the first to officially call his plane the Air Force One, when he upgraded to a jet, a Boeing 707. 10 years later, in 1963, a Swiss American aviation company, Learjet, introduced the first private aircraft with seats for six passengers and two pilots. Its price tag started at about $650,000. In the late '7s, Boeing began converting its popular 747 model for private luxury travel. Over the next two decades, charter services emerged, allowing people to easily explore and book private trips, ushering in a new era of flying. [Applause] Qatar has been building its own aviation empire since 1993 while using planes as a diplomacy tool. In 2000, the country sent a Boeing 747 to Iraq as an expression of solidarity. In 2013, when Turkish and Lebanese hostages were released from Syria, Qatar executive quickly offered jets to escort them home. And in 2018, Qatar's Amir gifted a Boeing 747 to Turkish President Arduan, citing his trust in Turkey. I don't think there's any question that they regard aviation as a kind of an integrated well fabric if you will, economics, diplomacy, uh, military power, so hard and soft power. They there's no way they could be spending this monster amount of money on jets without having a sense of what it all means. >> Becoming an aviation powerhouse has also helped Qatar secure its position in the Middle East. When tensions escalated between Qatar and its neighbors in 2017 over its ties to Iran, Egypt, the UAE, Bahrain, and Saudi Arabia imposed a massive blockade. They denied the tiny kingdom access to their airspace and seas and closed its only land border. Qatar relied on imports for survival and the country was facing a crisis. It quickly turned to its aviation superiority to flex some muscle. The airline flew thousands of dairy cows on Qatar Airways cargo planes from Germany, the Netherlands, the US, and Australia to Doha, where a new farm was waiting to help give the country more control over its food supply. >> Aviation was their lifeline. And whether economically or diplomatically, there was that high-profile story, I believe, of using Qatari jetliners to fly dairy cows into the country so they could have milk, you know. I mean, it was it was an exceptional moment in history. >> And Qatar's influence in aviation extends beyond just owning fancy planes. The Qatar Investment Authority, the country's $500 billion sovereign wealth fund, has bought shares in other airlines. In 2025, it scooped up a 25% stake in Virgin Australia after Australia wouldn't allow Qatar Airways to expand its flights. In 2020, Qatar secured its 25% stake in IA, which operates British Airways, Iberia, and Heirlingus among others. And back in 2012, the country bought a 20% stake in London's Heathro airport. It also owns part of two African airlines, Airlink and Rwanda Air. Qatar's ties to the United States also run deep. It shelled out $8 billion over the last 20 years to help develop and maintain the Al- Uid air base, the largest US military facility in the Middle East. It houses some 10,000 troops. In May 2025, Trump said Qatar pledged another $10 billion towards the base over the coming years. These closed ties benefit some US manufacturers as well, like Boeing. Qatar Airways has an order for 210 aircraft. And Qatar Executive flies 22 private jets made by another American manufacturer, Gulfream, with a few more scheduled for delivery next year. The private jet business is also growing worldwide. Today, there are about 23,000 private jets operating around the world, and about 65% of those are based in the US. That's up from 10,000 jets operating globally in 2000. Analysts say the global business jet market could reach nearly $70 billion by 2032. But owning a private jet isn't the only option. Some invest in fractional ownership, kind of like a time share for jets, where owners share costs such as maintenance, staff salaries, and hanger space. Others book and share chartered planes, or they use jet card programs that are like a prepaid card for flight hours. Vista Jet is one company that offers such a program. >> As long as the runway is long and safe enough, we'll make it happen. >> Vista Jet started with just two Lear jets in 2004. Now it has a fleet of more than 300 aircraft around the world. The company uses a subscription-based plan in which clients buy flight hours and it replicates ownership. But what it does is that it takes away all the ownership risks where the asset depreciation, the the management of the aircraft and the operation of the aircraft. >> It's a convenience clients are willing to pay for. The flight time costs anywhere from 11,000 to about 25,000 an hour depending on the type of plane. Members have to purchase a minimum of 25 flight hours a year with a 3-year commitment. And there's a separate fuel charge on top of the hourly rates. One flight from New York to London on this aircraft type could cost you $300,000 where our average client average net worth are well over half a billion dollars. members are guaranteed a plane will be available for their flight as long as they give 24 hours notice. >> It's like doing a jigsaw puzzle. How you make sure that you have enough availability, but at the same time, we can't have too much waste. >> To avoid empty seats, the company also sells individual chartered flights outside the membership program. And they're looking for ways to reduce their carbon footprint. Studies have found that private flights are up to 14 times more polluting per passenger than flying commercial as a carbon footprint is spread across fewer people. One study calculated that 2 hours of a private flight emits more carbon dioxide than an average person creates in a year. And because these planes fly higher, the emissions are released in more sensitive parts of the atmosphere where they linger, potentially contributing to more global warming. 85% of our customers opt in for our carbon emission program. They could give us, for example, a 7-day booking notice because it help us to organize our fleet to reduce that empty leg. like Qatar executive Vista Jet is competing to meet its customers high expectations for luxury when cooking on a private jet. Um it's very much the food has to be specifically engineered so that things don't dry out and that's what they really specialize on and getting the the food from worldrenowned chefs like Nou and things like this. On the 7,500 we have two ovens which are perfect for for this. With the the private airline, we can really cater to our clients specific needs. Um, we know exactly what our clients like. Um, and we take feedback after every single flight and we write back to our company. When it comes down to owning, sharing or chartering, experts say there's one simple principle. For those who travel a lot and can afford it, owning is perhaps the way to go.

A small sultanate whose Sultan is counted among the richest and most powerful people in the world. Sultan Hassan Al Bolkiah. But Brunai is not just a name for wealth, it is a sultanate where every path reveals a new secret. A place where the Sultan's will is the law and where the biggest powers of the world become silent before his orders. Once again flattered by the videos of ZM TV. Nazreen Brunai is a small but rich country in natural resources. Where the ruler Hassan Al Bolkiah has a personality that is a living example of rule, grandeur and power. He has been the sole owner of the throne of Bronai since 1967. And after the death of Queen Elizabeth II, he has now become the longest- reigning monarch in the world. This story is not just about time but about wealth that an ordinary person cannot even imagine. Several international sources have reported that Hassan Al Bolkiah's personal net worth is over $30 billion, and these are very conservative estimates. No one has been able to know about his actual wealth yet. Behind their grandeur and luxurious lifestyle are Brunei's oil and gas reserves which are hidden like gold under the ground. Every barrel of oil and every drop of gas adds another zero to the Sultan's wealth. But the Sultan of Brunei is not one of those royal families who hide their wealth. He has the world's largest private car collection, which astonishingly has 7000 cars. This is not an ordinary collection but it includes 600 Rolls, 450 Ferraris and 3880 Bentleys. And the newest piece in this collection is a 24 carat gold plated Rolls Royce Silver. Which has been recorded by the Guinness Book of World Records as the most expensive car in the world. A car that not only shines but also gives a glimpse of this expensive hobby of the Sultan. The Sultan's car collection is estimated to be worth around $5 billion. If we move away from ground travel and turn to the sky, then his private jet plane is no less than anyone. A gold plated Boeing 747400 worth around Rs 40 crore. But the Sultan uses it not as a ship but as a flying palace. The Sultan also gifted a private jet to his daughter as a birthday gift. In total, the Sultan has 17 airplanes in his private collection and gold has been used extensively in each of the planes. For this reason, the Sultan of Brunei is also called the Emperor of the earth and the sky. There are many other interesting aspects of Sultan's personality. As he is a certified pilot from the Royal Military Academy Sand Hast. And he calls his favorite barber from London to spend $21,000 on just one haircut. This is just a small sample of his luxurious lifestyle. The Sultan is also an art collector who purchased a painting by the famous French painter Renouart for $10 million. He is so lavish in spending that in 1996, he spent $30 million in one go on his 50th birthday party. In which Michael Jackson was paid $5 crore for his performance alone. During this period, a two-week holiday was observed across the country and a whole new stadium was built especially for this occasion in which 60 thousand royal guests from all over the world participated. But the real surprise was that the royal guests who came to the birthday party were given real gold medals. Talking about the palace, it was designed by Filipino architect Leandro Locsins. While the interior design was done by Kwan Chu. Who also designed the famous Zamana Burjul Arab. The construction of this palace in 1984 cost $5 billion. Exactly the same amount that was spent on the construction of Bur Khalifa. This palace, called Nurul Iman, is three times bigger than the Palace of Versailles in Paris. It has an astonishing 1788 rooms, 257 bathrooms, a banquet hall for 5000 guests , a mosque, a garage for 110 cars , an air-conditioned stable for 200 polo ponies and five swimming pools. And the most special thing is that its dome is made of 22 carat gold. Every corner of the palace tells the story of grandeur. Gold and marble floors, doors and wall carvings. There are more than 550 gold chandeliers and 18 elevators in this palace. It is said that the Sultan's palace is not a palace but a whole small city. This palace is called Istana Nurul Iman. It has a total of 17 floors and its total floor area is spread over 21 lakh square feet. Now here the question may be coming in your mind that how does the Sultan and his family travel from one place to another in such a big palace? Electric golf cards are used in the palace for this purpose. This palace is not only the home of the Sultan but it is also the Prime Minister's Office and the headquarters of the Government of Brunei. All the major decisions of the country are taken from here. Sultan Hassan al-Bolkiah is not only the richest king but he is also the Prime Minister himself. Finance Minister as well as Home Minister. The Constitutional Amendments of 2004 further strengthened their powers. They have been given a position beyond the law. Be it his official status or personal. Now after seeing all this you might be thinking that Brunai would be a liberal country. But it is not so. The most important aspect of his rule is the implementation of Sharia law. In 2019, the Sultan fully implemented Islamic law. Under this law, a person committing adultery and homosexuality is punished with death by stoning. Whereas there are punishments like cutting off hands for theft. The move became the target of severe criticism worldwide, with protests recorded outside the Sultan's luxury hotels in London and Paris. Not only is there a strict ban on alcohol and pork in Brunei, but smoking tobacco is also illegal here. Only government muftis can issue fatwas and making fun of their fatwas is also a punishable offence. Here it is mandatory for all men to participate in Friday prayers. And it is the duty of all women to wear their clothes in such a way that their body is completely covered. Despite all these restrictions, some things are absolutely free in Brunei. Like education and medical. There is no income tax of any kind here. This is the reason why many citizens here love their Sultan and call him the Sultan of the people. He believes that Sharia law has kept the country peaceful and has reduced the crime rate. But many people find this country very boring and they leave for the city of Mary in Malaysia for some freedom. Where alcohol and a free atmosphere are available. In 1997, Miss USA Shanoon Marketic sued Sultan and his brother. Claiming that she was held hostage and forced to commit indecent acts. But the US court did not give any decision against the Sultan due to sovereign immunity. Which shows that along with money, he is also a very powerful person. Apart from this, another scandal occurred in 1990 which is called Amend Crisis. Prince Jafri Bolkiah, the younger brother of the Sultan of Brunei, was accused of misusing billions of dollars of royal wealth. He spent money like water on luxury autos, private jets and thousands of expensive cars. This caused a significant damage to Brunei's reputation and also created tension within the Royal Family of Brunei. Eventually, the Sultan seized control from his brother and exiled him. Brunai is a sultanate where one part tells the story of luxury while on the other side strict Islamic law is applied. But apart from all this, there are some facts which you may have never heard about Brunai before. The word Brunai actually means that's it. Its full name is Negera Brunai Daru Salam. Where Negera means country and Daru Salam means a place of peaceful living. Oil and gas produced in Brunei drives 90% of the country's economy. Brunei was captured by Japan during World War II due to oil shortages and later liberated from Japan by the British. In 2013, Brunei became the first Asian country to ban shark finning. Brunei places a strong emphasis on education, with a literacy rate of 98%, better than many other countries. The currency here is Brunei Dollars which can also be used in Singapore. Kampong Ayer is also known as Venus of the East. An old settlement built over water on the Brunei River, it showcases the ancient culture of Brunei. needs a thorough inspection that takes about 3 days and costs around $40,000 every 24 months you're looking at a major inspection that can ground your aircraft for up to 6 weeks and cost half a million and if you need to replace an engine that's a cool $3 million each speaking of engines let's dive into something fascinating about jet engine maintenance these aren't like car engines where you can just change the oil and keep going every single flight hour is logged and tracked the engines have sophisticated sensors that record everything from temperature to vibration patterns if anything is slightly off you're looking at mandatory maintenance regardless of cost now let's get into fuel costs and this is where the numbers get really interesting a midsize jet Burns about 150 Gall per hour but that's at cruising altitude during takeoff you're looking at nearly double that amount and here's something most people don't realize FES the higher you fly the more efficient your simulator training every 6 months this means flying to specialized facilities and spending several days practicing emergency procedures each training session costs about $30,000 per pilot and if they fail they need to repeat the training and you need to find replacement pilots in the meantime then there's your flight attendant who needs special training in everything from emergency procedure to   5. @rtbrtb_dutchy4183 [1 likes]: This video is completely misinformation. 

I’ve been a corporate pilot for 25 years and am well aware of the actual cost. 

I worked for one company who bought a brand new jet for $23 million and sold it 15 years later for $16 million. 

Also, currently flying a Gulfstream 550 and the cost of operating is $4500 per hour. This includes EVERYTHING: fuel, maintenance, engine maintenance, crew pay, crew training, insurance. Etc etc. 

This is part 91, meaning not charter. So training is once a year. And no, training doesn’t cost $50K or $30K. And no, if a pilot fails, he doesn’t start all over. They usually get 1 or 2 extra sim session. Cabin crew are not required to have training at all. 

If you want to charter, a Gulfstream can cost you $15,000 per hour. Why the huge difference?  Well, the airplane needs to come to you first. And after it drops you off, it needs to reposition somewhere else to start another trip. So, if you charter from LAX to JFK, the airplane might need to come from Phoenix. And after you are dropped off, it might need to fly to Atlanta empty, for the next trip. Those empty flights cost money and need to be paid for. 

You don’t have that if you own your own aircraft. 

Also, I can’t remember the last time I paid $6 for a gallon. We basically been paying around $3 per gallon. But that doesn’t matter. The $4500 per hour includes the fuel.

Owning a private jet makes it much easier to travel between locations, meet with clients, and stay connected with your team. It saves a significant amount of time by avoiding commercial airport delays, gives you access to more airports, and lets you fly on your own schedule. For the right person, a private jet can be a powerful and worthwhile investment.

But only a few of them take their dreams to the runway. Today we are going to talk about a real Indian story of Shank Airlines. Shank Air founder Shravan Kumar Vishwakarma did not come from a corporate or aviation family. Shravan Kumar Vishwakarma's journey began with an ordinary middle class upbringing. Kanpur, Uttar Pradesh, where his childhood was not much academic and he was never considered among the top students in school. There was a time when he worked as a tempo driver. Initially, he earned money by driving autos and tempos and also tried some small businesses which often failed. But every failure taught him a lot about life, markets, and risk taking. He studied the aviation sector deeply 4 years ago. Understand the NOC process, DGCA rules and regulations. Worked on practical aspects of financing and operations. Only then did he check his vision and turn the idea of Shank Air into a real project. In 2014, he ventured into cement trading. From where his entrepreneurial foundation was built. After this he expanded his scope to TMT steel, mining and transport businesses. Where he built a truck flat. Over 400 trucks and a strong base financially and operationally from there. When he proposed the idea of Shank Air, the first problem he faced was funding. Investors considered the aviation sector risky and due to lack of experience, he was rejected many times. But Shravan's vision was clear - to make flying affordable and accessible to the common man of India. Especially connecting tier two and tier three cities. After many ups and downs, his hard work paid off. When Shank Airlines received the No Objection Certificate from the government and the airline was officially ready to enter the Indian aviation market

- Wanted Doctor, Became Airline Owner, Shock Story 😱✈️ Mohammad Haris: Kind: captions Language: en He wanted to become a doctor but ended up becoming an airline owner. The unbelievable journey of Mohammad Harris. Just imagine a boy sitting among his books memorizing pharmaceutical formulas. The next stop of his life should probably have been the lab of some big pharma company. A world of white coats, gloves, microscopes, and chemicals. But suddenly the story takes a turn. These scenes change that Sam boy is now standing amidst the roaring sound of aircraft engines. Airplanes parked on runways are now associated with his name and thousands of people are flying the world he created. Who could have imagined that a boy studying pharmacology would one day become the owner of an airline? This is the story of Mohammad Harris. And believe me, this is not just a business story. It is a journey of dreams. Destiny and determination. One that makes you ask again and again. How did this even happen? A calm locality in Kerala. A simple family, an ordinary upbringing. Muhammad Harris Tataril whose name is today taken with pride in the air travel industry once only thought about fulfilling his family's expectations, completing his studies and building a stable career. studying BA in history and economics followed by pharmarmacology clearly showed that his family's expectations for his career were quite conventional. The medical field is safe, respectable and financially rewarding. So why not choose that path? And to be honest, Harris himself thought the same. But life is never linear. It takes you where you truly belong, not where society pushes you to go. India of the 90 was changing. The vines of liberalization had begun to blow. Millions of Indians working in Gulf countries were constantly struggling with travel, flights, tickets, visas, and the hope of meeting their families far away. During this changing era, Harris probably asked himself for the first time, will I just do what everyone else is doing or should I do something that actually makes people's lives easier and also gives me my own identity. And from here begins that turning point when a medicallyincclined young man starts shifting his path toward the aviation and travel industry. Harris started a ticketing and tour operating company from Kerala and named it Al-Hind. At that time perhaps even he didn't imagine that this name would one day become a global brand. The beginning was small but the vision was big. This wasn't just about selling tickets. It was a support system for NRC Gulf workers, families, pilgrims, tourists, every person who dreamed of touching the sky. Gradually Al-Hind began winning people's hearts with its reliability, trust and service quality. When people started saying get your ticket done from Al-Hind and all tension is over, that was the biggest victory. Within just a few years, this small ticketing agency turned into an international travel company. By 1995, the company had reached the Middle East, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Oman, Kuwat. Wherever there was an Indian community in the Gulf, Al-Hind made its presence felt there. This was not just business expansion. It was an answer to people's needs. Millions of Indians work there. Live far from their homes. And during every vacation, every festival, every emergency, their first trust is always a reliable travel partner. And that partner became Muhammad Harris. But here's the important point. Harris didn't just run a company. He built an ecosystem. He understood that travel is not just a plane ticket. It is an experience. From booking to boarding, from visa to hotel, every step should be user friendly. That is why Haris gradually modernized his business model. He adopted technology. He observed that the world was moving online. So in 2014 he launched his B2B travel portal. Now not only consumers but travel agents also connected with him. Flight booking, hotel booking, visa services, everything on one platform. Then in 2020 he launched Dhanhind Utility Private Limited for fintech and travel utility services. This is the kind of mindset great entrepreneurs have. When the world was struggling with the pandemic, the aviation industry was collapsing and many airlines was shutting down. That's when Harris thought differently. He saw opportunity even in collapse. He said if the world is changing then we must change too. But stopping is not an option. And from here began the planning of that big dream which is now becoming a reality. A new airline named Al Hinder. Just imagine how incredible this journey is. From selling tickets to flying planes, from passenger service to plane ownership, from the ground to the sky. Such stories are rare. And the most inspiring thing is that Harris does not come from any big industrialist family. He has no political backing, no inherited aviation empire, just a vision, hard work and people's trust. And this trust became so strong that today the turnover of the Al-Hen group is more than 20,000 cr. This is no small achievement. It proves that if your direction is right, no matter how small your beginning is, the destination can be enormous. Now let's talk about the dream that is about to become reality. Al-H hinder competition in India's aviation industry is increasing. Lowcost airlines have brought the skies closer to people. But now it's time for regional connectivity. It is time to connect small cities. Providing an affordable aviation experience to people in tier 2 and tier three cities will be the real gamecher. And this is exactly where Al-Hinder is entering. The government has granted them an ANOC which means it is no longer just a dream. The path to actual airline operations is now open. The specialty of this airline will not just be that it will fly. Its specialty will be that it will be run by a company that has already earned people's trust in the travel industry. Harris understands what a passenger needs. on-time flights, fair prices, good service and respectful treatment because he has understood the travel industry from the ground level. He has seen from the beginning what kind of troubles passengers go through, how difficult ticketing can be, how stressful cancellations are, how much anxiety a delayed flight creates. That is why his airline will not just be about machine operations. It will be a human-centric airline. Many people will say luck supported him. But luck supports only those who try. If Haris had followed a conventional path at that time. Perhaps today his name would be limited to a file in some medical department. But he dared to dream. And the dreams that come true are those for which a person sacrifices sleep, faces difficulties, takes risks, falls, gets up and keeps moving. The success story of Alhin proves that someone truly understood the pain of the Indian community working in the Middle East and turned that understanding into a business vision. Where others only saw money, Haris saw people's needs. And when need and business insight come together, the result is something immense, an empire.
