- How Aircraft Leasing Works & Why Airlines Do It: Kind: captions Language: en how aircraft leasing works and why airlines do it we're used to assuming that if an airline paints an aircraft using its livery and operates it it must also own it the topic of aircraft leasing does not get the attention it deserves given its vitality and prevalence to the airline industry the incredible financial burden of obtaining brand new aircraft is so significant that virtually all airlines decide to lease at least some of their aircraft let's dive into the functionality of aircraft leasing if an airline wants to obtain a new plane they can either buy it or lease it let's imagine that qatar airways is willing to expand its operations and needs a new fleet of 10 boeing 787s to do so if they have enough cash or can obtain bank loans they can purchase the aircraft directly from boeing a list price for one brand new 7878 is around 240 million dollars so assuming qatar airways would get a good discount of 35 from boeing purchasing 10 brand new 787s would cost them 1.56 billion dollars the other option that qatar airways has is to lease those 787s for which the lease rate average sits at around 1 million dollars a month if qatar airways decides to retain cash and chooses to lease they can reach out to firms that specialize in commercial aircraft leasings such as avalon or assuming that qatar airways will also receive a discount of from g-cass they now only have to pay 78 million dollars per year for all 10 of the 787s if we imagine that the leasing contract will last for around 20 years its total value would be around 2.34 billion dollars in that way gcass can make a profit by leasing their airplanes while qatar airways manages to reduce its cash expenditures and retain liquidity in its business there's a huge variety with regards to types of leases and deals that airlines can make with the lessers firstly we should distinguish between wet dry and damp leases an example of a dry lease where a commercial airline takes aircraft from the leasing company for a set period of time in most cases the aircraft remains with the leasing company and the airline operates it using its own crew it's the most commonly known type of lease wet leases also known as acme occur more ad hoc when an airline demands additional aircraft to fill the gaps in their network the name acme acmi stands for aircraft crew maintenance insurance as all are provided by the lesser demand for acme deals was particularly strong once a portion of the global 787 fleet became grounded due to their trend engine issues these are usually short-term and total contract values are much less significant than those of dry leases damp leases particularly popular in the uk are when the letter like air belgium provides the aircraft flight crew and maintenance while the lessee provides the cabin crew other deals can take place between airlines and aircraft lessers a famous example is a sail and lease back deal those occur subsequently through aircraft purchased by the airline when said carrier sells its planes to a leasing firm and leases them back the most common reason for such transaction is to free up capital and obtain cash agreements as such happen quite regularly historically one such instance saw qatar airways make a sale and lease back deal for seven brand new a350s with gcas in 2017. we also saw cathay pacific and bok aviation make a sale and lease back deal covering six boeing triple seven 300 er jets just last month financially troubled carriers are more likely to do such deals as cash becomes more valuable for their short-term survival over the coming months we could see a significant amount of such deals taking place their size will only be constrained by the financial stability of the leasing companies with airlines desperately grasping for cash leasing firms with strong books can score some very beneficial contracts under normal circumstances leasing allows airlines to reduce their indebtedness helps with cash retention and greater financial agility paying a small fraction of the aircraft's value each month is a vital part of many airline strategies it's the only fleet financing solution that allows a dynamic expansion without huge capital expenditures which many airlines have recently benefited from whiz air wouldn't have achieved its impressive 253 growth in passenger numbers over the last eight years if it had at least 100 of its fleet recent dynamic growth of lot polish airlines indigo gold or fuelling was also fueled by aircraft leases these carriers now have over 70 of their planes leased if leasing provides airlines with so many benefits why would any airline even consider purchasing aircraft on their own well actually owning aircraft makes sense too firstly once the airplane gets grounded either due to a network planning issue or a global pandemic the airline doesn't have to continuously pay for its lease as it owns it outright additionally purchasing aircraft directly from manufacturers usually results in a lower total cost per aircraft obtained in times like today airlines such as lufthansa which owns eighty percent of its fleet can encumber their aircraft that is to obtain loans using their aircraft as collateral this option is so vital given current circumstances but many carriers globally currently do not have such an option if you like this video please like and subscribe to the simple flying channel and be sure to click the notification bell

- When You Should Buy The Jet: Financial Advisor Explains: Kind: captions Language: en Flying private costs $80,000 from New York to LA. If your first reaction is that's insane, this video is for you. If your first reaction is that doesn't sound too bad, this video is really for you. Most people think private aviation is a billionaire's thing. They're wrong, but the majority people that can afford it have the math wrong. They're asking the wrong questions, they have the wrong math, and the reality is they're either spending money they shouldn't or they're leaving behind money that they wish they would have spent. So here's what we're going to cover. In the next 10 minutes, I'm going to share with you the exact numbers behind flying private. Who it makes sense for and who it doesn't. We're going to look at the three most common ways to fly private, chartering, jet card, or going out and buying a jet. Once we talk about the numbers, we're going to talk about the benefits. And these benefits, they have nothing to do about financial outcomes. They're all the intangible benefits that come with flying private. But when we think about those benefits, there also comes with costs. I'm going to reveal to you the one cost that has nothing to do with dollars that you have to be thinking about if you're going to be flying private. It's the one thing that I warn clients about as they go down the path of flying private. And that's going to lead us to the framework that I use with the clients that we work with at Moment Private Wealth to decide is flying private something that fits inside their financial picture. Let's dive in. Now, most of these conversations start with what's the cost. And although cost is a determining factor, it shouldn't be the end-all be-all. Whenever we're thinking about cost, we always break it down into the three ways that you can fly private. Because the reality is whenever you're flying private, depending on how you're flying could largely determine the overall cost of flying private. Is it a one-time expense? Is there ongoing expenses? Is there a big down payment you have to put down? All these are factors that you need to be thinking about when you're flying private. So, what are the three ways that you're typically going to see private aviation come into play for families? The first is looking at chartering. Think about chartering as the lowest commitment way to get into the private aviation game. You decide you're going to take your family on a vacation from St. Louis to Cabo. I live in St. Louis, Missouri, and there are no direct flights to Cabo. It is a complete pain to get to. And you say this is a once-in-a-lifetime vacation, we want to charter. So we're going to fly private. So we're going to have a direct flight from St. Louis all the way down to Cabo. And when you're chartering, you're going to have a cost per hour. Typically, this is going to range somewhere between $8,000 and $14,000 an hour. It can range upwards of 20 or 30 thousand dollars an hour if you're on a really big or heavy jet, but for the sake of the conversation today, let's assume it's somewhere in that $8,000 and $14,000 an hour range. So you can pretty easily calculate what that's going to look like based on the amount of time that you're going to be actually flying in the air. So if you're going on a trip and you're thinking about what is that actually going to cost to charter a plane, then think about it as the amount of time from wheels up to wheels down and apply those ranges that I just mentioned, that $8,000 and $14,000 per hour. Now, why would somebody charter versus getting a jet card? Well, there's very low commitment, right? You want to go on on that vacation and you decide that we're just going to dip our toe in the water from a private aviation standpoint. This is a great way to dip our toe in the water. Now, if you start to fly with a charter and you realize I'm really loving flying private, I want to do this more frequently, it's going to make sense to ramp up to a jet card. Because whenever you start to have more and more commitment to flying private, the cost that you're going to have per hour is going to go down when we jump into a jet card. And that jet card, as I highlighted here, it's the most common because think about it as the middle ground. We're going to get to reduce our our hourly costs. They're typically somewhere between six and nine thousand dollars an hour, but we're going to have this upfront cost that we're going to incur because we're going to have to put down $250,000, maybe $500,000 into a jet card. Think about it like a really fancy gift card that you bought. Then you're going to have to utilize it over the course of a certain number of months or years. And the biggest benefit here is the convenience factor. So when you go to charter a plane, you're going to have to find that jet plane that you're going to charter, it's got to be available, the crew's got to be available on the dates that you're going. That's why the cost per hour is going to be higher when you're doing that one-time charter. But on these jet cards, it's going to reduce the overall hourly rate that you're going to be paying. But this is truly for somebody who's gone beyond dipping their toe in the water and they know that they want to continue flying private on a more regular basis, but they haven't put themselves in a situation that they're going to go all in and they're going to buy their own plane. And now, when we think about people who are going to be buying their own plane, the biggest thing that I'm thinking about is the number of hours that we're going to be flying and can we afford it? When we think about the number of hours that we're going to be flying, if we're flying anywhere under 100 hours, it does not make sense to buy. If we're doing anything over 200 hours, it normally makes sense to look at potential ownership. But when you think about ownership, money cannot be a factor. We're going to be paying millions of dollars for a plane, and then normally you're going to have a million to three million dollars of cost throughout the course of the year. And when we think about ownership, there are some benefits, right? People talk about the tax benefits, you can write the entire jet off. This can be a huge benefit from a business owner perspective, but there's a huge amount of cost. We do not want to go spend millions of dollars a year, potentially tens of millions of dollars, on a plane simply to get a tax write-off. But if we're flying hundreds of hours a year for our company, it could make sense to pull the trigger on the ownership. So those are the three ways that you could fly private and exactly what those costs look like. Now, there's a wide range here. We're talking about tens of thousands of dollars up to millions of dollars. But the amazing thing is about flying private, all of these give you the same types of benefits. What are those benefits? Let me share them with you. I get to see this all the time with the clients that we work with at Moment. The number one benefit that I see for clients that we work with and folks just generally in private aviation is convenience. And when we think about convenience, I think about it in terms of two areas. Number one is our time convenience. When you think about flying on a commercial airliner, you're going to have to drive to the airport, you're going to park at the airport, you're going to go through TSA, you're potentially going to check your bag, you're going to go to the gate, you're going to get there early because the flight is leaving on time with or without you. Then you're going to fly, you're going to get your bags. There's a lot that goes into that entire process. It's not just wheels up to wheels down. So when you think about the time comparison of flying commercial versus the time comparison of flying private, it's roughly half the amount of time because you're able to drive straight to the airport, out on the tarmac, you get your bags out, put your bags on the plane, and you take off. When you land, your bags come out, there's going to be a car waiting for you, put your bags in the car, go to your location. So it's going to save you 50% of the travel Now, when I think about 50% of the travel time, a lot of people like to say, "Well, my time is really, really expensive." But for that math to make sense, your time needs to be really, really expensive. We're talking about tens of thousands of dollars per hour expensive. And there's people that that makes a lot of sense for, but the vast majority of Americans, even entrepreneurs that are really successful, it doesn't make sense. And when you think about the time component, yes, you need to understand what is your time cost, but that's very rarely the name of the game. The most likely situation from a convenience factor is that you own a business and you're flying into locations that are very hard to get to. We work with a family where they are flying to all these small-town locations all across the United States. They are impossible to get to. They would have to fly into a major hub, drive 90 minutes in a car, drive 90 minutes back, get on the plane, fly back home, and it increases their travel time by multiples, two or three X. And they're flying their entire team there. And so from a convenience factor, that can start to make a lot of sense. And this could be applicable to all three of the ways to fly private that I mentioned before. You don't have to dive headfirst in and buy a jet, start with chartering, go to the jet card, and then potentially that ownership piece. When we think about the other benefits of flying private, one of the most common ones that come up is as a business tool. And when I think about it as a business tool, there's two primary tools that I think about. Number one is entertainment, entertaining clients. And when I think about entertaining clients, you really need to be in a business that this is an expectation. It needs to be normal for the business that you are in to entertain clients and have them be flying private. I think the reality is that for a lot of businesses, they underestimate that it could be a huge negative to flying private. For example, if one of our vendors called me and they said, "Luke, we really appreciate the relationship that we have with Moment. We'd like to fly you on a private jet down to Miami and we want to do X, Y, and Z with you. Would you would you like to come?" My first reaction would be, "Am I overpaying that vendor?" And that might just be for me, but I think that's something that a lot of business owners need to be aware of is if you're not in the space of flying private, a lot of times you think that flying private could be this huge unlock from a business development standpoint, from an entertainment standpoint with current clients, but I think it's a tool that you need to be very careful with. But if you're in an industry that that's expected, it can be an amazing opportunity to be able to utilize that tool. I would encourage you to tread lightly because depending on who you're taking on that plane, it could actually have negative effects. And the last piece is thinking about if you have a family. And when I think about my own family, I have three kids, that would be an amazing unlock. But is it an unlock that's worth 10, 20, 30 thousand dollars an hour? For me, it's not. For other folks, it is. This cost that's not on an invoice is the cost that it potentially have on your kids by flying private with them. And I've seen it go both ways. I've seen kids grow up flying private and then be in a situation that they understand their parents put in a lot of hard work and that allowed their family to fly private. And once they grow up, they get out of college, they fly commercial just like 99% of us. But I've also seen it go the other way where flying private set this expectation that that's just what we do. And it set a poor expectation because those kids, as they grew up, they didn't realize the amount of work, the amount of effort it took to be in a situation that they could fly private. So if you are looking at flying private, I would encourage you to just be aware of this. And I think there's no perfect formula to it, and I think a big, big factor is just your kids in general. How do you think your kids are going to be affected? You know your kids better than anybody else, so I'd encourage you to think long and hard about that before you start putting them on a private jet and flying them private. Because once you go down that path, it's a very hard to go back. Now, who should be doing this and who shouldn't be doing this? I was talking to a friend, not a customer, the other day and we were talking a little bit about flying private and he was talking about the cost of it. He made this comment. He said, "Well, Luke, I would never fly private because if it cost $150,000 to fly round trip to the location that I'm going to. If I were to invest that money at a 7% interest rate, it's going to give me $580,000 over the course of the next couple decades." And when I looked at him and I said, "That means that you should not take that trip." If you're doing those math calculations, it's a good indicator that you probably can't afford to fly private. And if you are doing those calculations, the only channel that I would look at is potentially chartering. Maybe you're going on a once-in-a-lifetime vacation and you want to fly private. There's nothing wrong with that. And as I think about flying private and doing a way that you're chartering, it's a one-time expense. So, if we're looking at any big one-time expenses, whether it's a vacation or whether it's a just the actual flight itself, you just need to bake it in your budget. Does it align with our basics? What is our income? What are our expenses? And what are we trying to save throughout the course of the year? If we can hit our savings goal that aligns with our financial plan by doing that one-time charter, I would say go for it. But, do not do it at the detriment of your financial plan. Now, the other folks that we're looking at flying private for are going to be the jet cards and looking at buying. Who should be utilizing a jet card? The folks that we should be using a jet card are the ones that feel really strongly about private aviation. They've decided, they've done a couple charters, they've decided we really want to fly private, but we don't want to dive headfirst into ownership, either from the standpoint of we're not flying enough hours or we don't have the funds. We don't have the financial capacity to go out, buy a plane and be able to afford the maintenance on that plane. And a jet card could be a really good option. Similar to option number one with the chartering, I would bake this in your budget. Cuz we're talking about a potentially $250,000 expense. This goes back to the basics. expenses? And are we in a situation that we can hit our savings goal while still adding this expense in? Now, what I would encourage you to think about is am I going to be flying private permanently or is this just a stage of life that we're in? Because if you are going to be flying private permanently, even in that jet card, that middle column, you're going to be in a position that you're going to have to save significantly more money for retirement to be able to afford this new lifestyle cost that you've incurred. Because for a lot of families, I see them spending 10 to $20,000 a month on their lifestyle expenses. And if we're talking about adding a jet card, we might be talking about doubling or even tripling our lifestyle spend just on flying alone, which could be 100% fine, but make sure you're baking that into what the long-term plan is. And then the last piece is ownership. Who should be buying a plane? For the folks that are buying a plane, I have two rules of thumb. Number one is can you write it off in your business? So, what that means is that if you buy a plane, you can write off 100% of it in year one. You buy a $10 million plane, you get a $10 million tax deduction. It can be an amazing financial outcome. But, when you actually run the numbers, you look at everything behind the scenes, it is not a good financial decision. And if you're trying to buy a plane, do not try to do financial engineering. There is no way to make the math make sense. It's a great luxury and if you can afford to do it, great. I would also encourage you to be thinking about the number of hours that you're going to be flying. Typically, if you're going to be buying a plane, that plane needs to be in the air 2 to 300 hours a year to be in a situation that that makes sense. Now, if we do some quick basic math here, if you're flying 300 hours a year, that means that you're flying 150 days 2 hours in the air. That is a lot of days. That's every other day you are on that airplane flying it. And so when you think about ownership, that's why a lot of businesses it could potentially make sense if they have other members of their team, not just themselves, flying on that plane on a regular basis. So, we've covered it all today. We've covered the real cost, we've covered the benefits, we've covered who should be doing this and who should be flying private and that number one cost that no one talks about. Now, what should you do with this information? I would encourage you, digest it, think about it, and if you have questions, schedule a call below. We talk about this with a lot of the entrepreneurs that we work with at Momentum Private Wealth. - How to Buy an Airplane and Write it Off on Your Taxes: Kind: captions Language: en Nobody likes taxes—they stink. But here, taxes are very important to help people&nbsp; discover their aviation lifestyle potential. You’d be amazed at what’s possible for helping you&nbsp;&nbsp; structure ownership in a tax-advantaged&nbsp; way for that airplane of your dreams. So why are we talking about taxes today? Well, this is the area where many clients who&nbsp; are interested in being involved with aircraft,&nbsp;&nbsp; discover things they had no idea were possible. Many clients come in planning to buy an&nbsp; airplane with the money they have on hand,&nbsp;&nbsp; and then they discover that there are&nbsp; ways to structure business or ownership&nbsp;&nbsp; of the aircraft that could generate direct&nbsp; offsets to their personal income for taxes. Literally, you can end up owning the plane and&nbsp;&nbsp; paying less in taxes than&nbsp; you would have otherwise. Now, there are many details that go into this,&nbsp;&nbsp; and of course, I’m not a lawyer,&nbsp; a CPA, or even a tax advisor. However, I am a businessman and have spent 20&nbsp; years helping people discover these things and&nbsp;&nbsp; working with our advisors, so I do know a lot. The&nbsp; big-picture concepts are the most important part. The first thing to understand&nbsp; is that, in the United States,&nbsp;&nbsp; our tax law is actually very well-structured&nbsp; to support a desire to enjoy private aircraft. This is due to the business tax laws that&nbsp; give us access to what’s called depreciation. There are multiple types of depreciation,&nbsp; but they all have the same idea:&nbsp;&nbsp; giving us a way as a business to&nbsp; invest in something and write off&nbsp;&nbsp; that investment either upfront&nbsp; or in a very accelerated way. There’s something called bonus depreciation—that’s&nbsp; the one that’s so exciting because,&nbsp;&nbsp; depending on which tax year we’re in,&nbsp; it could be anywhere from 40, 60, 80,&nbsp;&nbsp; even 100% in years past, where we can take&nbsp; the entire purchase deduction immediately. Now, we’re not talking about the&nbsp; operating costs in that moment. When we say bonus depreciation, that means&nbsp; if we buy a million-dollar asset that is&nbsp;&nbsp; 100% used for business in a particular tax year,&nbsp;&nbsp; it could range from a million dollars being offset&nbsp; of regular income to maybe 60% of that income. It depends on the set of facts we’re working with. Then there’s something called&nbsp; Section 179 depreciation and&nbsp;&nbsp; also general accelerated depreciation. It really doesn’t matter what any of these things&nbsp; are called. What’s most important is to understand&nbsp;&nbsp; that there are ways to structure the actual&nbsp; purchase price so that it becomes a tax lever. That’s the big-picture concept. How it gets applied to which type of&nbsp; aircraft and which type of ownership&nbsp;&nbsp; structure is the nuance of how to&nbsp; best structure it for each client. Many people considering aircraft think&nbsp; that they have to have a business in&nbsp;&nbsp; order to enjoy business deductibility for an&nbsp; aircraft. And while that’s technically true,&nbsp;&nbsp; what they’re not aware of is all the&nbsp; different ways that actually owning&nbsp;&nbsp; an aircraft and putting it in different&nbsp; structures creates an actual business. One of the things we did at LifeStyle Aviation&nbsp;&nbsp; was to create our own sharing&nbsp; programs, called DiamondShare. This has been a successful program&nbsp; for us for, oh gosh, 15 years. Owners own the aircraft, members fly the aircraft,&nbsp;&nbsp; and pay for general access. Their flight&nbsp; hours become business hours for the owner. It’s a fabulous structure for enjoying the&nbsp; aircraft, structuring the ownership cost,&nbsp;&nbsp; and allowing the owner to enjoy maximum&nbsp; deductibility with minimum usage of the aircraft. Another structure we use&nbsp; often is called a leaseback. When we say leaseback, we&nbsp; generally mean to a flight school. Looking at leasebacks is a great idea&nbsp; and can sometimes be the ideal situation,&nbsp;&nbsp; because technically, these structures can produce&nbsp; profits, making it a profitable enterprise. Now, candidly, we don’t promote&nbsp; this as “buy an airplane,&nbsp;&nbsp; you’ll make money.” You might&nbsp; make money, but what’s great&nbsp;&nbsp; about it is setting up this potential for&nbsp; profitability within a business structure. This means you’re now going to enjoy&nbsp; all the tax-deductible potential of&nbsp;&nbsp; aircraft ownership for business purposes. Those that fly the plane pay the school&nbsp;&nbsp; for access, and every one of&nbsp; those hours is business use. Every one of those business-use hours drives that&nbsp; deductibility for the owner of that aircraft. Another variation of a leaseback concept is where&nbsp; the aircraft is put in a Part 135 structure. Part 135 has unique rules. It’s the same idea, but the usage profile of the&nbsp;&nbsp; aircraft is managed by an entity in the&nbsp; business of providing charter services,&nbsp;&nbsp; flying that aircraft around and providing&nbsp; those charter services to individuals. Then that income flow comes back to the&nbsp; owner—a great business structure that can&nbsp;&nbsp; provide possible profitability&nbsp; and definite deductibility. Another ownership structure many people&nbsp; think of is partnerships. It’s a valid&nbsp;&nbsp; structure, especially if you happen to have&nbsp; a brother-in-law, a family member, or someone&nbsp;&nbsp; you already know you want to split an aircraft&nbsp; with. This can be an ideal structure for you. There’s flexibility in how it can be split for tax&nbsp;&nbsp; purposes, and there are unique ways it&nbsp; can be created, structured, and managed. Partnerships are typically the&nbsp; hardest to create and get into,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the hardest to get out of. We help clients&nbsp; conceptualize partnerships and implement them. We also provide guidance on the considerations&nbsp;&nbsp; they’d want to think about to determine&nbsp; if it’s the right structure for them. If you’re considering an aircraft right now, now&nbsp; is the time for you to dig into this information&nbsp;&nbsp; and find out if it could apply to your situation.&nbsp; And if so, which tax year should it be applied to? Take, for example, if it’s November. There’s time for you to acquire an aircraft, get&nbsp; full deductibility before the end of the year,&nbsp;&nbsp; and take full advantage. It might&nbsp; be the ideal time to do it, or you&nbsp;&nbsp; can plan for another tax year—whichever&nbsp; is exactly right for your circumstances. Having helped hundreds of people discover&nbsp; an aviation lifestyle and achieve it,&nbsp;&nbsp; I can honestly say that it is&nbsp; one of the most life-changing&nbsp;&nbsp; things you can possibly do if you&nbsp; have the inclination to want it. Let us help you discover it, explore it,&nbsp; and find ways to justify and pay for it.&nbsp;&nbsp; It’s doable, and nothing can replace what&nbsp; an airplane offers in private aviation. So we hope you explore it, discover&nbsp; it, attain it, and enjoy it!

Buy a plane for $1m  and do 100% depreciation then sell the plane for $800k  that $800k is now one big lump of income with a probably a tax bill.
  2. @tfargher1 [9 likes]: Only works when you have enough profits to effectively take the deduction.  You can take the loss against profits but the loss can only be taken against your basis in the company.  If you put nothing in you can't write anything off against it as you have no basis to take the loss.  Once basis is gone you can't take further loss deductions.  So make sure you have enough profits to take the deduction and or basis.

- Why Jet Engines Need Single-Crystal Blades | Explained Simply: Kind: captions Language: en Let's start with one of the most remarkable innovations, the single crystal blade. A tiny component that takes on a massive challenge. It may look small, but this little blade faces extreme temperatures, immense forces that could tear metal apart, and constant stress every single second. Here's the problem. Most metals are made of millions of tiny crystals stitched together with weak spots called grain boundaries where they meet. Under high heat and stress, these boundaries become failure points causing creep where metal slowly stretches and fatigue where tiny cracks grow until something breaks. So, how do engineers fight back? with single crystal technology. A game-changing process where molten metal is cooled in just the right way to form a single flawless crystal. No grain boundaries, no weak links. That makes these blades incredibly strong, resistant to creep, fatigue, and thermal stress. They don't stretch, they don't crack, and they hold their shape even when spinning at thousands of RPMs in searing heat. On top of that, the single crystal structure conducts heat more efficiently, keeping the blade cooler and boosting engine performance. In short, without this one innovation, modern fighter jet engines wouldn't survive more than a few minutes in the air. Watch the full video. Click the link in the description and watch the complete video now. And if you're enjoying the content, support the channel by buying me a coffee. It really helps.

difference between procurement and purchasing let's start the video procurement and purchasing are the two terms that are often used interchangeably if you ask a layman about the difference you may get a reply that purchasing and procurement are one and the same but if you ask the same question to a manager you'll get a significantly longer answer as to how and why there's a world of differences between purchasing and procurement let's discuss what is procurement procurement refers to the process of identifying shortlisting selecting and acquiring suitable goods or services from a third-party vendor through direct purchase competitive bidding or tendering process while ensuring timely delivery of the right quality and quantity what is purchasing purchasing is the set of functions associated with acquiring the goods and services that an organization requires purchasing is a small subset of the broader procurement function this process includes activities like ordering expediting receiving and fulfilling payment lets explains the differences between procurement and purchasing the end goal of procurement is to identify the company's needs and fulfill the procurement of those needs it is a strategic and proactive process on the other hand the end goal of purchasing is to arrange company expenditure and buy goods and services for the company it is a reactive process in procurement places more importance on an item's value than how much it costs but in purchasing more focused on price than value procurement involved in the end-to-end activities that needed to acquire all necessary goods and services procurement does everything from need recognition to sourcing contract closure and record keeping purchasing gets involved when it's time to buy goods and services tasks involved in purchasing our ordering expediting and payment procurement focused on developing long-term and a win-win relationship with suppliers on the other hand purchasing focused on making efficient transactions not overly concerned with developing vendor relations if you want to read in details or download the pdf go through the link in the description like share the video and - Day In My Life: CONTRACT PILOT! | What It's REALLY Like!: Kind: captions Language: en This is a day in my life as a contract pilot flying a turborop all over North America. got used be sure to wipe them down and then I want you to wipe down all the windows as well nothing tackier than having a bunch of fingerprints now before you get started you know when you're loading everything on the airplane one thing I would suggest is to wipe down all your surfaces before passengers get there especially if you were not the last one on the aircraft you never know what got left prior so it's really good to just go through and check all the surfaces beforehand and then always make sure to wipe things down afterwards even if you think you are you know going to be the one working the plane the next day just be sure that you are leaving the aircraft absolutely spotless because you never know things could change and you could be taken off a trip or another flight attendant is going to be coming in so you do not want to leave any mess whether that is for yourself or for another flight attendant the next day for most of the airplane I do like to use the microfiber cloth but when it comes to cleaning the laboratory I don't want you to use that microfiber cloth I want you to use something disposable so that after you wipe down everything in the lavatory including the sink and the toilet that you can throw this out I don't want you to use the microfiber cloth to you know wipe down the whole airplane then the lavatory and think oh I just need to wipe something else down and you grab that microfiber that you just wiped the toilet with and you wipe something out so put your gloves on grab some paper towel and your spray and then get going in the bathroom and be sure to throw all of that out before you touch anything else now for the mirror I will grab a brand new paper towel and wipe all of that down separately from the paper towel that I use to clean the toilet fold the ends of the toilet paper in a nice little triangle and there's other ways to get even fancier with this but this is just a basic fold to make things look nice then be sure to empty the trash either Midway through flight if it gets too full or especially at the end always leave a fresh trash bag for the next flight all right now that you have the airplane entirely clean there's going to be items that you are going to give to the line Personnel now sometimes you can get these to them right away and then get the rest of the airplane clean or clean it all up and then hand them over depending on whatever works for you so today I didn't have too many Linens I just have some napkins so I put it in a laundry bag and I'm going to hand this over I usually do label this before I hand it over so they don't remember what airplane this goes to and then also I have my dish bucket I'm going to hand this down to them it is labeled as well and then I'll just tell them hey this is what day we're headed back out most times you know they can get dishes done within a couple hours as far as laundry goes I do like to wait until I either make it back to my home base or if I know that I'm going to be somewhere for more than 24 hours because sometimes laundry it gets dry cleaned and it'll often take much longer so I don't want you to get caught without your laundry if it's a quick turn um you know hang on to this but you use your judgments as far as how long you're going to hang on to the laundry I also keep a much larger bag for like blankets so after every Charter I do take the blankets off and I have those dry cleaned I do blankets that are specifically for charter flights and I have other blankets that are specifically for the owners and I do keep those separate so the owner's stuff stays much nicer but that's what I am handling remember that is what I hand over to the line guys and then hand over your trash as well one thing that you can hand over to the line guys also or line Personnel is if you have a cooler bag fill that up with all your leftovers that you want to bring back onto the airplane maybe your your milk your juice your lemons limes that kind of thing hand them a cooler have that labeled as well

Um, I it's just more logistically makes more sense and it's cheaper. Uh, so when you really start using it more than 200 hours, then you should buy an airplane. And why would you consider buying pre-owned versus straight from the manufacturer? >> Well, the first reason is because you want instant gratification. You need something now. Uh that's the most uh obvious reason because when you want to go to manufacturer today, it could be a one, two, or even threeear wait for an airplane. The other thing is is because airplanes depreciate. Okay, take COVID out because that was where a freak anomaly in the market, but they're depreciating assets. And usually in a normal marketplace, an airplane will probably depreciate between 30 and 50% the first five years, just like a car. And um and there's nothing wrong with it. Your warranty goes away after 5 years, but there's a big difference in price. So people want to measure, you know, do I want instant gratification and get a lower investment. So, it's all much more complicated and and they need the help of a management company that has that global coverage. But, um, to buy the airplane, basically, you're doing a letter of intent. It was brand new. It was a totally different concept. And I'm curious if you when you reflect back what you've learned now. You know, if you would what you would do differently or how you think it's changed the industry because it probably has. >> I mean, I learned how what people really want right off the bat. So when you're talking to somebody on the phone or through emails, people usually are a little overexaggerating what their needs are and you know they're flapping their wings. They tell their pilot, I want to spend 25 million. I need to go 10 people and go, you know, eight hours. And then when you sit in front of the person with our app and you actually see, it's like a doctor. If you don't tell me your real problem, I can't solve your problems. So once they see what we can do, if they don't give me the real information, they won't get a real answer. when they see that they start saying, "Well, I don't really want to spend 25. I really want to spend 15. I don't really carry more than four people. I really need to go four hours." So, what we do is we really narrow down what the minimum airplane model they should have and then they can get anything above that. And I've learned through this whole experience of really what the client wants and what they need. And I think that helps us solve their issues much quicker and you don't have to waste a lot of time looking at stuff that you know you're not going to end up with. Um, and it's the same thing. Why would you You're putting your life, your your executives lives, your family lives in this airplane. Uh, why would you want to buy the cheapest? I mean, I I'd want to want the best pedigree, you know, the best maintenance history. Cosmetically, you don't have to worry about that kind of stuff. You could change, but you can't change history. So, that's the one thing I think people really should pay attention to.

  14. @Justice-Always-Wins: The Illusion of Wealth: How Liquid Capital Hoarding Creates Cartels, National Debt, and Economic Servitude


At the core of modern economies, power is no longer driven by production, but by the strategic withholding of capital. When liquid assets — that is, immediately available money — are hoarded by corporations, banks, political elites, and financial institutions, they create a hidden yet powerful cartel system. These players don’t need formal agreements — their unified behavior around hoarding money creates the same effect: centralized control of cash flow and widespread societal dependency.

Liquidity Cartels: The Occult Economy of the 21st Century
In a world where money no longer circulates but stagnates at the top, true economic power belongs not to the producers but to the gatekeepers of capital. Around major corporations, financial entities, and political parties, a shadow network emerges that controls:

Who gets access to funding?
What kind of education is considered “useful,”
Which industries receive support, and
What ideas are economically “viable”

In this model, capital becomes the arbiter of the future. These financial actors function as creditors of entire nations, shaping not only markets but public narratives, government policies, and cultural values.

And because political campaigns, regulatory bodies, and even media outlets are funded by these same interests, what the public sees, hears, and is taught is carefully crafted to protect the flow of money into the few — and out of the many.

Education as Cartel Programming: Training the Labor Force for Debt-Based Societies
As public education evolves to meet “market needs,” it subtly becomes a tool for economic conformity. New “digital skills,” “competency frameworks,” and “career paths” are not designed to promote self-sufficiency or ethical innovation — they’re designed to serve creditor demands.

That’s why education doesn’t teach:
How to be self-reliant,
How to generate value outside corporate structures,
Or how to build economic systems rooted in community, sustainability, or cooperation.
Instead, it trains you to fit in, to work for those who own the capital, and to seek survival by borrowing from the very system that impoverishes you.

Cash as a Concentrated Power Mechanism
The dogma of “cash is king” works in tandem with liquidity hoarding to create the illusion that cash equals safety. But real wealth lies in land, tools, community access, time, and knowledge, not digits on a screen.

If money were allowed to flow freely, society would create, trade, rebuild, and heal. But because cash is withheld at the top, governments are forced to borrow, and people begin to hoard out of fear, worsening the cycle. Scarcity becomes a mindset, not a material reality.
And this is no accident. It’s an engineered dependency that feeds inflation, stagnates innovation, and erodes civil liberty.

The Path to Social and Spiritual Reform
What if withholding money wasn’t seen as “good business,” but as economically damaging behavior? What if idle capital were taxed, while money in circulation was rewarded?

Here’s what a reform might include:
Double-taxing idle cash (since it contributes directly to inflation and poverty),
Reducing or eliminating property taxes and replacing them with liquidity-flow taxes,
Rewarding capital use, not storage — encouraging reinvestment and sustainable productivity,
Guaranteeing a social baseline income or equity, not as charity but as a return on national participation,
Preventing foreign investors from draining domestic capital without recirculating or reinvesting locally.

This would:
Free governments from foreign debt dependence,
Revive the middle class,
Turn wealth into a socially regenerative value, not just a statistic.

Conclusion: Financial Liberation Requires Spiritual Clarity
The greatest power of modern capital cartels is not their money, but their ability to make people believe they are worthless without it. Break that belief, and people will stop chasing the system and start rebuilding their world with meaning, cooperation, and real wealth.
“Wealth is not cash. Wealth is what you can freely convert, create, and pass on.”
Once this becomes common knowledge, no central bank, government, or corporation could enslave the masses through scarcity ever again.
And when money becomes a servant to people — not their master — healing can begin.

- Exact Steps to Buying Your First Airplane: Kind: captions Language: en i've owned my cessna 182 for six years now and it has absolutely changed my life i've gotten to go on flying adventures to the of assembling that project so step one is to determine whether you ought to buy an airplane rent an airplane or join a flying club now while buying an airplane might sound awesome it might not renting probably need a slicker name for that but it comes down to freedom frequency and finances essentially renting is usually easier on the finances side of this because it's predictable and you 116th share providing access to private jets without the full financial burden jet card memberships also offer flexibility with pricing based on hours flowing making them an ideal option for those who fly less frequently but still want the convenience of private Aviation these options provide an affordable way to experience the luxury of private jets without the full responsibility of ownership regardless of flight hours and variable costs which depend on usage together they determine the annual expense of owning and operating a private jet fixed costs include Cruise salaries hanger fees insurance and pilot training a two-pilot crew typically earns between $200 and $400,000 annually depending on the Jet's size and complexity hanger fees vary by location with costs ranging from $30,000 of jet fuel per hour while midsize and heavy Jets consume 300 to 500 gallons per hour with fuel prices averaging $5 per gallon in 2025 a 5-hour Coast to Coast Flight can cost between 2500 and 122,00 menu complexity and passenger requirements unscheduled repairs or upgrades while less predictable should also be factored into the budget despite these expenses private jet ownership remains appealing
